Prioritize corrective actions
Track actions to closure
controls.riskAddressed: Control effectiveness not independently assessed; systemic issues not identified; problems missed by first line oversight
controls.testingApproach: Verify assurance plan exists and is current; review audit execution records; confirm findings documented with supporting evidence; check corrective actions tracked and closed

controls.controlName: Assess Equipment Reliability and Identify Chronic Failure Issues
controls.controlStatement: Who: Reliability Engineer, Maintenance Manager, Equipment Engineer | What: Analyze equipment failure data and maintenance history to identify chronically unreliable equipment ('bad actors'); assess failure modes and contributing causes | When: Periodically (at least annually) as part of maintenance optimization; when equipment shows patterns of failure | Where: Reliability analysis reports, failure trend documentation | Why: To identify equipment requiring enhanced attention; to prioritize reliability improvement efforts | How: Compile failure history and maintenance records; analyze failure frequency and patterns; calculate Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF); identify repeat failure modes; assess root causes (design, maintenance, operation); rank equipment by reliability risk | Evidence: Reliability analysis documentation showing failure trends, identified 'bad actor' equipment, failure mode analysis, improvement prioritization
keyActivities: Compile failure and maintenance data
Analyze failure frequency and patterns
Calculate reliability metrics (MTBF, MTTR)
Identify repeat failure modes
Assess root causes of failures
Rank equipment by reliability risk
Recommend improvement actions
controls.riskAddressed: Chronically unreliable equipment continues with high failure rate; unplanned downtime; barrier reliability degraded
controls.testingApproach: Review reliability analysis for completeness; verify failure data analyzed; confirm bad actor equipment identified; check improvement recommendations documented

controls.controlName: Implement Reliability Improvements for Identified Problem Equipment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Reliability Engineer, Equipment Specialist, Maintenance Manager | What: Develop and implement improvement plans for identified unreliable equipment including design modifications, maintenance strategy changes, or operational controls | When: Following reliability assessment; improvements prioritized by risk and impact | Where: Equipment modification documentation, updated maintenance procedures, operational controls | Why: To improve equipment reliability and availability; to reduce unplanned downtime and maintenance costs | How: For each bad actor equipment, develop root cause-based improvement plan (design change, maintenance optimization, operational controls); implement improvement; measure effectiveness through failure trend monitoring; adjust approach based on results | Evidence: Reliability improvement plans with baseline metrics, implemented modifications or procedure changes, post-improvement failure trend data showing improvement
keyActivities: Develop improvement plan for each problem equipment
Analyze root causes and address in plan
Implement design modifications if needed
Update maintenance strategy if needed
Implement operational controls if needed
Monitor post-improvement reliability
Adjust approach based on results
controls.riskAddressed: Unreliable equipment continues without improvement; unplanned downtime continues; improvement opportunity missed
controls.testingApproach: Verify improvement plans developed for bad actor equipment; check modifications or procedure changes implemented; confirm post-improvement reliability monitored and shown to improve

controls.controlName: Develop Equipment Care Strategy Based on Criticality and Reliability
controls.controlStatement: Who: Equipment Engineer, Maintenance Planner, Reliability SME | What: Develop maintenance strategy for each equipment item based on criticality to production and process safety, failure modes, and reliability requirements | When: During equipment specification/selection; updated per reliability reviews | Where: Equipment Care Strategy documentation, maintenance plans | Why: To ensure maintenance effort is appropriately focused on critical and high-failure-risk equipment | How: Assess equipment criticality (impact of failure on production, safety, environment); identify failure modes and consequences; select appropriate maintenance strategy (run-to-failure, fixed-interval PM, condition-based); document strategy rationale; ensure strategy resources are available | Evidence: Documented Equipment Care Strategy for critical equipment, maintenance strategy selection rationale, allocated resources, strategy implementation plan
keyActivities: Assess equipment criticality
Identify failure modes and consequences
Evaluate failure risk magnitude
Select appropriate maintenance strategy
Document strategy rationale
Identify required resources
Establish performance metrics
controls.riskAddressed: Maintenance effort not focused on critical equipment; low-risk equipment over-maintained; critical equipment under-maintained; maintenance strategy inappropriate for risk
controls.testingApproach: Review equipment care strategies for appropriateness of strategy to criticality; verify resources allocated; check that critical equipment has proactive maintenance approach

controls.controlName: Execute Asset Care Plan and Monitor Performance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Maintenance Technician, Maintenance Planner, Operations Manager | What: Execute planned maintenance activities per developed strategy; track completion; monitor equipment performance and condition to verify strategy effectiveness | When: Continuously throughout asset lifecycle | Where: Maintenance system, work orders, equipment performance data | Why: To maintain equipment in operational condition; to deliver promised reliability and availability | How: Schedule maintenance per strategy; execute work per procedures; track completion and findings; collect performance data (uptime, failure rate, repair frequency); compare actual performance against strategy targets; adjust strategy if performance does not meet expectations | Evidence: Completed maintenance work orders, equipment performance data showing uptime and reliability metrics, maintenance completion rates tracking against schedule, strategy effectiveness assessment
keyActivities: Schedule maintenance per strategy
Execute maintenance work orders
Collect performance data
Track maintenance completion
Monitor equipment uptime and reliability
Compare performance to targets
Adjust strategy if needed
controls.riskAddressed: Maintenance not executed per strategy; equipment performance deteriorates; reliability targets not achieved; unplanned failures occur
controls.testingApproach: Verify maintenance scheduled and executed per plan; review equipment performance data; compare actual performance to strategy targets; confirm adjustments made if performance inadequate

controls.subProcess: Local Management System Development and Control
controls.controlName: LMS Document Owner Assignment and Accountability
controls.controlStatement: Who: Site Process Owner (SPO) or equivalent | What: Assigns document owners and establishes accountability for content ownership of LMS sections and controlled documents | When: Upon LMS creation and annually reviewed as part of management system health assessment | Where: Local Management System documentation hierarchy and org structure records | Why: Ensures clear ownership prevents content drift, inconsistency, and accountability gaps; maintains LMS as living system aligned with actual work execution | How: SPO designates responsible person/role per LMS section with written sign-off; documents in org responsibility matrix; tracks via LMS custodian dashboard | Evidence: Responsibility matrix with dated assignments, SPO approval records, LMS health dashboard showing owner assignments current
keyActivities: Identify all LMS sections and controlled documents requiring owners
Assign document owners close to work execution (not centralized custody)
Document assignment in responsibility matrix with roles and titles
Establish review cycle expectations and update frequency per owner
Verify owner competence and authority to approve changes
Track ownership changes and transitions during reorganization
Report LMS ownership health via quarterly management review
controls.riskAddressed: Lack of clear ownership leads to outdated procedures, conflicting guidance, and gap between LMS documentation and actual work practice; creates audit findings and regulatory compliance risk
controls.testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) all major LMS sections have named owner with written assignment; (2) owner role aligns with actual business function; (3) owner approval records exist for recent document updates; (4) ownership changes are formally approved per Management of Change; (5) LMS dashboard/records show current vs. overdue ownership assignments

controls.controlName: Systematic LMS Review and Update Cycle
controls.controlStatement: Who: LMS Custodian/Focal Point and SPOs | What: Conduct systematic review of LMS content on defined cycle; identify gaps and updates needed vs. business objectives and regulatory changes | When: Minimum 2-3 year review cycle for all content; priority areas (SEAM Standards, critical safety procedures) reviewed annually | Where: LMS repository system; review meeting minutes; update prioritization log | Why: Prevents obsolescence and inconsistency between documented processes and actual work; ensures Group and regulatory requirements operationalized; demonstrates management commitment to continuous improvement | How: (1) Annual stakeholder meeting to identify changes in business context, regulations, SEAM Standards, incident learnings; (2) Risk-prioritize update scope; (3) Create update plan with owner assignments and timeline; (4) Track completion via management review dashboard; (5) Communicate changes to affected staff | Evidence: Annual review meeting minutes with attendees, documented change drivers, prioritized update list, completion status reports, sign-off by Asset Manager
keyActivities: Schedule annual LMS health and relevance review meeting with SPOs and Asset Leader
Document changes in business context, regulations, and SEAM Standards implementation
Review prior audit findings and assurance recommendations for LMS gaps
Identify sections requiring update based on risk and business priority
Create update plan with owner, scope, timeline, and resource allocation
Assign accountability for each update to relevant SPO or process owner
Track completion of updates and document in controlled repository
Communicate implemented changes to organization with training where needed
Report LMS update status and completion % in quarterly management review
controls.riskAddressed: LMS becomes stale and disconnected from actual work, regulatory requirements drift unchecked, SEAM Standards implementation incomplete, audit findings accumulate, management system loses effectiveness and credibility
controls.testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) documented annual LMS review meeting with agenda and participants; (2) documented change drivers and regulatory updates addressed in review scope; (3) risk-prioritized list of sections requiring update; (4) LMS update plan with owner assignments and completion timeline; (5) evidence of completed updates (date, owner, approval); (6) sample recent updates checked for accuracy and communication to staff; (7) management review minutes showing LMS status reported

controls.controlName: MoC Process Owner Appointment and Accountability
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager (or delegate) | What: Appoints MoC Process Owner (typically Technology/Engineering Manager) with explicit accountability for MoC effectiveness; documents appointment and authority | When: Upon asset commissioning or organizational change; formal appointment documented in org structure | Where: Org structure documents, MoC procedure, Asset Manager signed appointment memo | Why: Establishes clear accountability for ensuring MoC process functions per SEAM Standards, prevents uncontrolled changes, ensures hazard assessment and risk mitigation before implementation | How: Asset Manager issues appointment memo specifying: (1) MoC Process Owner role and responsibilities; (2) authority to approve MoC procedures and policy; (3) accountability for assurance and effectiveness review; (4) reporting line and escalation path; (5) competence requirements | Evidence: Signed appointment memo with date, Asset Manager signature, job description reference, documented delegation of authority, MoC procedure signed by appointed owner
keyActivities: Identify candidate for MoC Process Owner role (typically Engineering/Technology lead with credibility)
Define MoC Process Owner responsibilities and authority in writing
Issue formal appointment memo signed by Asset Manager
Ensure appointed owner understands SEAM Standards MoC requirements and best practices
Verify owner competence in process hazard analysis and risk assessment
Establish reporting cadence and escalation path to Asset Manager
Document appointment in org chart and communicate to asset staff
Conduct annual review of MoC PO performance and effectiveness
Plan succession and backup for MoC Process Owner role
controls.riskAddressed: Without clear accountability, MoC process lacks leadership and becomes ineffective; changes bypass proper hazard assessment; risks are not identified/mitigated; incidents result from poorly managed modifications
controls.testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) current org chart shows named MoC Process Owner with reporting line to Asset Manager; (2) signed appointment memo exists with date and Asset Manager approval; (3) job description/procedure shows MoC PO responsibilities match SEAM Standards requirements; (4) MoC PO can articulate key responsibilities and process effectiveness; (5) evidence of MoC PO leading process effectiveness reviews, assurance, and improvement; (6) MoC procedures are formally approved by appointed PO with signature and date

controls.controlName: Management of Change Procedure Compliance Assurance
controls.controlStatement: Who: MoC Process Owner and MoC Process Focal Point | What: Conduct periodic quality assurance of initiated MoCs to verify adherence to 8-step process and risk assessment procedures; sample changes at initiation and approval stages | When: Continuous monitoring via MoC tracking tool; formal audit at least quarterly with minimum 10% of initiated MoCs reviewed | Where: MoC tracking system/tool; MoC quality assurance audit checklists; assurance report to MoC PO | Why: Ensures changes do not bypass hazard assessment and risk mitigation steps; verifies competent staff involved; detects process gaps early; prevents uncontrolled changes reaching implementation | How: (1) MoC Focal Point monitors all initiated changes in system for completeness of scope statement; (2) Quarterly, PO selects representative sample of MoCs (risk-stratified: all High-risk, sample Medium, limited Low); (3) Review against MoC procedure checklist covering Steps 1-8, hazard screening, risk assessment quality, approvals; (4) Document findings in audit report; (5) Report to Asset Management and escalate non-compliance | Evidence: MoC quality assurance audit checklist, signed audit report with findings and recommendations, evidence of management response to findings, tracking of corrective actions
keyActivities: Define MoC quality assurance criteria based on SEAM Standards 8-step process
Develop audit checklist covering process steps, hazard screening, risk assessment, competence, approvals
Establish sampling plan (e.g., 100% High-risk, 30% Medium, 10% Low)
Conduct quarterly MoC audits by MoC Focal Point or independent reviewer
Document findings: scope completeness, hazard identification quality, risk assessment rigor, approval authority
Identify root causes of non-compliance (training gap, resource constraint, procedure clarity)
Report findings to MoC Process Owner and Asset Manager
Implement corrective actions (procedure update, training, coaching)
Track closure of audit findings and effectiveness of corrections
controls.riskAddressed: Process Safety risk: changes proceed without adequate hazard screening or risk assessment; incompetent personnel involved; risk mitigation actions not implemented; scope changes approved without re-review
controls.testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) documented MoC quality assurance plan and checklist exist; (2) MoC audit is performed at least quarterly with documented sample selection and scope; (3) audit checklist covers all 8 MoC steps and SEAM Standards requirements; (4) audit findings documented with evidence (e.g., missing hazard screening, unsigned approvals); (5) root cause analysis performed for non-compliant MoCs; (6) corrective action plan issued and tracked; (7) management review minutes show MoC assurance results discussed; (8) sample recent MoCs checked to verify compliance with identified corrective actions

controls.subProcess: Control of Work Procedure Development and Maintenance
controls.controlName: Control of Work Procedure Documentation and Version Control
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset HSSE Lead and Process Owner for Permitted/Non-Permitted Work | What: Establish and maintain formal Control of Work procedures covering both Permitted and Non-Permitted Work; define hazards, controls, Step 7 Conversation requirements, SWC verification, and supervision expectations | When: Documented procedures in place before work execution; reviewed and updated minimum annually or upon organizational/operational change; version control enforced | Where: Local Management System repository; Permit Vision (for Permitted Work procedures); documented in asset CoW procedure manual | Why: Provides clear operational guidance ensuring consistent execution of work controls; establishes expectations for Step 7 Conversation, SWC verification, and supervision; reduces risk of hazards not being identified or mitigated at point of work | How: (1) Develop comprehensive CoW procedure addressing high-risk activities in scope (confined space, hot work, isolation, excavation, etc.); (2) Define roles: work performer, permit holder, supervisor, SWC verifier; (3) Specify Step 7 Conversation and SWC verification requirements; (4) Include hazard identification criteria and control hierarchy; (5) Document in LMS with version control, approval, and review schedule; (6) Communicate changes to all affected staff with training | Evidence: Dated and signed CoW procedure with Asset HSSE approval, version history, training records showing staff competence, procedure accessibility in workplace (electronic and hard copy where needed)
keyActivities: Conduct hazard assessment of all work activities in scope (permitted and non-permitted)
Identify high-risk activities requiring Start Work Checks (SWC) and Step 7 Conversation
Draft CoW procedure addressing: work identification, hazard assessment, control selection, supervision, SWC verification
Define roles and competence requirements: work performer, permit holder, supervisor, SWC verifier
Specify Step 7 Conversation requirements: when, who leads, what is discussed
Include escalation process for when work is unsafe to proceed or conditions change
Document procedure in LMS with clear formatting, intent statement, and life-critical steps highlighted
Obtain approval from Asset Manager/HSSE Lead and document date
Implement training program for all staff involved in work execution
Maintain version control and communicate updates via Management of Change
controls.riskAddressed: Without clear procedures, hazards are not consistently identified, controls are not verified at point of risk, supervision is inconsistent, incidents occur due to lack of clarity on safe work practices
controls.testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) formal CoW procedure document exists in LMS with dated approval; (2) procedure covers all in-scope high-risk work activities per SEAM Standards and asset operations; (3) procedure includes hazard identification, control hierarchy, role definitions, Step 7 Conversation, SWC verification; (4) procedure specifies supervision span of control and competence expectations; (5) version control evident (version number, date, approval history); (6) procedure is accessible to work teams (electronic and hard copy); (7) staff can articulate key elements of CoW procedure and their role; (8) training records show staff competence on procedure before work authorization

controls.subProcess: Step 7 Conversation and Start Work Check Execution
controls.controlName: Structured Step 7 Conversation and SWC Verification Before Work Execution
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work supervisor or lead (for Step 7 Conversation); competent SWC Verifier from work party | What: Conduct structured Step 7 Conversation covering: what can go wrong, what barriers/controls are in place, what if barriers fail, what has changed; verify all Start Work Check items completed and physical controls in place before work begins | When: Mandatory for all RAM Severity 4+ work and high-risk activities; conducted immediately before work starts (at point of risk) | Where: At job site/location where work will occur (not office-based); documented in permit record or work log | Why: Ensures all personnel understand hazards and control measures, catches last-minute changes or missing controls, creates psychological safety culture where people speak up about concerns | How: (1) Assemble work party at job location; (2) Conduct Step 7 Conversation using conversation card: what can go wrong, what controls in place, what if controls fail, what has changed; (3) All team members contribute and acknowledge understanding; (4) SWC Verifier independently checks SWC checklist against actual site conditions; (5) Verifier confirms physical controls visually present; (6) If any control missing or concern raised, work pauses and escalated to supervisor; (7) Once all checks green, supervisor/permit holder authorizes work to start | Evidence: Completed Step 7 Conversation record (permit record or work log entry), signed SWC checklist with verifier identity and date, supervisor authorization to proceed, documented any barriers or concerns raised and how addressed
keyActivities: Identify work activities requiring Step 7 Conversation (all RAM Severity 4+)
Schedule Step 7 Conversation for timing immediately before work starts
Assemble work party at job location with permit holder, supervisor, workers, and SWC verifier
Lead Step 7 Conversation: discuss what can go wrong, barriers in place, what if barriers fail, what has changed
Encourage all team members to participate; create psychologically safe environment for raising concerns
Document conversation participants and key discussion points
SWC Verifier independently reviews applicable SWC checklist
Verifier walks through site and visually confirms all physical controls are in place
Verifier makes Go/No-Go decision and documents on SWC checklist
If No-Go, work pauses; concern communicated to supervisor and escalated
Once Go decision confirmed, permit holder or supervisor authorizes work to proceed
Retain records per asset records management policy
controls.riskAddressed: Workers may not understand hazards or how to execute safely; last-minute changes to site conditions not detected; psychological safety lacking (workers do not speak up about concerns); barriers not actually in place despite paperwork; incidents due to gaps between planning and field execution
controls.testingApproach: Auditor observes or reviews recent work execution: (1) Step 7 Conversation completed with all required participants at point of risk (not off-site); (2) conversation card or work log documents discussion of: what can go wrong, what controls in place, what if barriers fail, what has changed; (3) all team members present and engaged in conversation; (4) SWC checklist completed independently by designated verifier; (5) verifier conducts physical verification at site (not from office); (6) all control items on SWC checked as in-place; (7) if concerns raised, documented how escalated and resolved; (8) supervisor/permit holder authorization recorded before work starts; (9) records retained per policy; (10) staff can articulate purpose of Step 7 Conversation and their role in ensuring work is safe to start

controls.controlName: Knowledge Management Focal Point Appointment and Trusted Content Governance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager or SEAM Leadership Lead | What: Appoints dedicated Knowledge Management (KM) Focal Point and establishes KM governance framework; defines accountability for organizing and validating trusted content in Single Point of Access (SPoA) and community forums | When: Upon SEAM Standards implementation; formalized in org structure; governance reviewed annually | Where: Org structure documentation, KM procedure, appointment memo, SPoA governance records | Why: Ensures systematic organization of critical knowledge, prevents information fragmentation, enables rapid access to trusted expertise and lessons learned, builds organizational learning capability and reduces repetition of mistakes | How: (1) Identify and appoint KM Focal Point with knowledge management experience; (2) Define responsibilities: SPoA maintenance, content moderation, community activation, LFE integration; (3) Issue appointment memo with authority and accountability; (4) Establish governance framework for content curation and validation by subject matter experts; (5) Document in SPoA Content Management Guide; (6) Provide training on KM tools and platforms (SIGN forums, SPoA, LFE, Expertise Finder); (7) Establish cadence for content review and expert validation | Evidence: Signed appointment memo with date, written KM governance procedure, SPoA access logs showing regular moderation, documented expert validation of critical content, training records
keyActivities: Identify candidate for KM Focal Point role with experience in knowledge organization and community engagement
Draft KM governance framework defining roles (KM FP, SME validators, community leaders)
Issue formal appointment memo signed by Asset Manager or SEAM Leadership
Define accountability for SPoA maintenance, content moderation, and expert validation
Establish process for identifying and tagging expertise areas (processes, risk areas, disciplines)
Create content management guide for categorizing and storing trusted knowledge
Set up moderation process for SIGN forums to ensure quality answers and peer review
Link LFE lessons learned to relevant expertise areas for easy discovery
Maintain Expertise Finder Master List (EFML) with current role and appointment changes
Conduct quarterly review of KM metrics (community participation, content usage, learning adoption)
Provide ongoing training and support to community leaders on KM platforms
controls.riskAddressed: Knowledge becomes siloed and inaccessible; duplicate problem-solving wastes resources; lessons learned are forgotten and mistakes repeated; external expertise goes untapped; organizational learning capacity is weak; SEAM Standards implementation slows without accessible guidance and best practices
controls.testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) KM Focal Point appointed with signed memo and documented authority; (2) KM governance framework and content management guide in place; (3) SPoA is actively maintained with current content moderated by named reviewers; (4) expert subject matter experts identified and appointed in EFML; (5) SIGN forums monitored with documented answers/discussions; (6) LFE lessons learned tagged and categorized per governance framework; (7) community network meetings held with documented attendance and KM discussion topics; (8) staff awareness of where to find expertise (SPoA, SIGN, Expertise Finder); (9) metrics show community participation and content reuse; (10) KM tools accessible and documented (SPoA, LFE links, SIGN forum links available to staff)

controls.controlName: In-Depth Incident Investigation Decision and Investigation Capability
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager or Operations Lead | What: Establish decision criteria and authority for determining which incidents require in-depth causal learning investigation; maintain competent investigation team (Community of Practice) capable of performing causal-reasoned investigations | When: Documented decision criteria established at asset commissioning; investigation decision made within 72 hours of incident reporting; competence assessments conducted annually | Where: Asset procedure for investigation decision criteria, Investigation Community of Practice roster, investigation sponsor appointment documentation, causal learning training records | Why: Ensures high-learning-value incidents receive appropriate investigation depth; prevents under-investigation (repeating mistakes) and over-investigation (resource dilution); builds organizational capability to identify and address root causes systematically | How: (1) Establish written decision criteria for in-depth investigation (Significant Incidents always; high-potential incidents assessed for learning value; recurrence history; risk profile); (2) Document in investigation decision procedure with clear authority and escalation path; (3) Identify and document competent investigation team members; (4) Ensure team has completed Causal Learning Fundamentals training and maintains competence; (5) Appoint investigation sponsor (senior manager with authority); (6) When significant incident or high-potential incident occurs, convene decision team within 24-48 hours; (7) Assess learning value using documented criteria; (8) Approve investigation scope and resource allocation | Evidence: Written investigation decision criteria and procedure, signed by Asset Manager; roster of competent investigators with training dates; investigation sponsor appointments for initiated investigations; decision meeting minutes showing criteria applied; causal learning training completion records; investigation scoping documents
keyActivities: Develop clear decision criteria for in-depth investigation (SI always; HPI assessed; recurrence; risk assessment)
Document decision process and authority in asset procedure
Identify pool of potential investigators and assess causal learning competence
Arrange Causal Learning Fundamentals training for investigation team members
Appoint investigation sponsor (Operations Lead or senior manager) with authority and authority
Establish investigation decision team composition and meeting cadence
Create incident reporting and notification process to trigger decision team assessment
Develop investigation scoping template defining ToR, team composition, timeline
Allocate minimum 50% FTE capacity for investigation team during active investigations
Conduct annual competence assessment of investigation team members
Document lessons learned from each investigation and update KM platforms (LFE)
controls.riskAddressed: High-potential incidents and Significant Incidents investigated superficially, root causes not identified, similar incidents repeat; organizational learning capacity weak; resources wasted on low-value investigations; knowledge loss when experienced investigators leave; failure to identify systemic causes allows drift toward major incident
controls.testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) documented incident investigation decision criteria and procedure in place with Asset Manager approval; (2) criteria clearly distinguish Significant Incidents (always investigate), High Potential (assess learning value), routine incidents (minimal investigation); (3) recent incident records show decision team convened within 72 hours with documented assessment against criteria; (4) documented investigation sponsor appointment for each in-depth investigation; (5) investigation team members have completed Causal Learning Fundamentals training (check training records); (6) investigation community of practice roster maintained with names, competence levels, availability; (7) investigation scoping documents (ToR) are complete and signed; (8) investigation timeline and resource allocation documented; (9) examples of completed investigations with documented root cause analysis and causal threads; (10) high-value learnings documented in LFE with cross-references to investigation findings

controls.controlName: Establish HAZOP Schedule and Obtain Leadership Commitment
controls.controlStatement: Who: HEMP Process Owner, Asset Manager | What: Establish schedule for 5-year HAZOP studies and obtain leadership approval to allocate resources | When: At asset lifecycle start and annually for review | Where: HEMP Plan, Asset Management System | Why: To ensure compliance with 5-year revalidation requirement and prevent gaps in process safety management | How: Document schedule in HEMP Plan, identify units with RAM Red/Yellow 5A/5B risks, obtain leadership handshake on resource allocation | Evidence: Signed HEMP Plan with schedule, resource allocation statement, documented leadership approval
keyActivities: Identify all assets/units with RAM Red or Yellow process safety risks
Schedule HAZOP studies to meet 5-year revalidation requirement
Obtain leadership approval and resource commitment
Allocate budget and personnel for HAZOP execution
Document timeline with key dates for planning and completion
controls.riskAddressed: Non-compliance with 5-year HAZOP requirement; loss of clarity on current process safety risks; hazards not identified timely
controls.testingApproach: Verify HEMP Plan exists and current; confirm leadership approval signature; validate all due units identified with scheduled dates

controls.controlName: Collect and Review Process Safety Information Before HAZOP
controls.controlStatement: Who: HAZOP Facilitator, HEMP Coordinator | What: Gather and review technical documentation, MOC records, maintenance status, and operational history | When: 9-12 months before HAZOP workshop | Where: Asset documentation system, GSAP/CGSAP records, MOC database | Why: To ensure HAZOP based on current accurate information and identifies recent changes introducing new hazards | How: Conduct systematic review of PSI, MOC documents since last HAZOP, maintenance status, SIF PFD data, previous assurance findings | Evidence: Checklist of gathered documents, MOC summary log, maintenance status report, document review meeting notes
keyActivities: Collect all technical design documents and P&ID updates
Review MOC documents opened/closed since last HAZOP study
Compile DOW/IOW exceedance records for the unit
Gather preventive and corrective maintenance status
Extract SIF PFD and demand rate data from testing results
controls.riskAddressed: HAZOP based on outdated information; failure to identify hazards from changes; gaps in barrier reliability understanding
controls.testingApproach: Verify document checklist complete; confirm all MOCs since last HAZOP reviewed; validate maintenance status current; review PFD records recent and valid

controls.controlName: Approve HAZOP Approach Based on Risk Assessment Criteria
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Safety TA2 or HEMP SME | What: Evaluate and approve HAZOP approach (New, Update, or Sample Review) based on documented criteria | When: Early in planning cycle, minimum 12 months before HAZOP execution | Where: HEMP Plan, HAZOP approach decision document | Why: To ensure appropriate HAZOP scrutiny matches risk profile and change history | How: Apply evaluation criteria (robustness of previous HAZOP, MOC process effectiveness, barrier validity status); document decision rationale; obtain multi-disciplinary team approval | Evidence: HAZOP Approach Decision Document with evaluation scoring, TA2/SME approval, documented justification
keyActivities: Assess robustness of previous 5-year HAZOP quality
Evaluate MOC process effectiveness and documentation quality
Review HSSE Critical Equipment and Barrier validity status
Assess regulatory or standard changes since last HAZOP
Compare findings to criteria matrix and select approach
controls.riskAddressed: Inappropriate HAZOP scope selection; wasted resources on unnecessary full updates; missed hazards from insufficient review depth
controls.testingApproach: Verify evaluation criteria checklist completed; confirm TA2 signature on approach decision; validate approach aligns with documented risk factors

controls.processArea: AIPSM Management of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers
controls.controlName: Identify HSSE Critical Equipment and Human Barriers from Hazard Analysis
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager, HEMP Focal Point, Hazard Analysis Facilitator | What: Review hazard analysis to identify equipment and human barriers critical to managing RED/YELLOW 5A/5B risks; compile preliminary list | When: Following completion or update of Hazard Analysis | Where: Hazard Analysis documentation, HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers list | Why: To identify barriers requiring special management focus due to role in preventing major accidents | How: Systematically review identified scenarios; identify valid barriers; determine if equipment (HSSE CE) or human action (HSSE Critical HB); document justification; assess barrier practicality | Evidence: Compiled preliminary list of HSSE CE and HB with process safety justification, barriers documented with scenario linkage
keyActivities: Review Hazard Analysis results for all RED/YELLOW 5A/5B scenarios
Identify all valid barriers for each scenario
Classify barriers as HSSE Critical Equipment or Human Barriers
Document justification for HSSE Critical classification
Assess barrier practicality and validity
controls.riskAddressed: Critical barriers not identified; barriers misclassified; unreliable barriers designated HSSE Critical; wrong controls focused
controls.testingApproach: Review preliminary list for completeness against scenarios; verify each barrier linked to at least one RED/YELLOW scenario; confirm classification appropriate

controls.processArea: AIPSM Management of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers
controls.controlName: Conduct Technical Authority Assurance of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Safety TA2 or equivalent authority | What: Independently review and provide assurance on completeness and correctness of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers list | When: After completion of Step 1, before management handshake | Where: Technical assurance checklist, HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers documentation | Why: To ensure list is technically robust, complete, and consistent with hazard analysis before management approval | How: Use assurance checklist to verify each item; assess completeness against scenarios; validate definitions and justifications; check consistency with standards; provide approval | Evidence: Completed assurance checklist, technical authority approval signature, documented findings and recommendations
keyActivities: Review list for completeness using assurance checklist
Verify each HSSE CE and HB meets definition criteria
Check consistency with Hazard Analysis results
Validate technical accuracy of identifications
Provide approval or recommend modifications
controls.riskAddressed: Incomplete/inaccurate lists proceed to management; flawed identifications not caught; loss of assurance credibility
controls.testingApproach: Verify TA2 conducted review; review assurance checklist completeness; confirm approval signature present; validate recommended modifications addressed

controls.controlName: Gather AIPSM Performance Data and Leading Indicators for Management Review
controls.controlStatement: Who: Production Unit Manager, Process Engineer, Data Analyst | What: Systematically collect and compile AIPSM performance data including design, technical, operational, and leadership integrity metrics | When: Quarterly at minimum for unit level; annually for asset/business levels | Where: AIPSM data dashboard, reporting system, performance records | Why: To provide leadership with current visibility of barrier health and identify trends requiring attention | How: Collect data from multiple sources (maintenance system, incident reports, KPI dashboards, assurance findings); compile into visual format (heat maps, metrics); trend data over time; identify outliers; prepare summary for management review | Evidence: Completed AIPSM performance data reports, data trending analysis, visual dashboards showing current status and historical trends
keyActivities: Collect leading and lagging indicator data from authoritative sources
Compile barrier health metrics from maintenance and operational data
Trend indicators over time to identify patterns
Create visual representations (heat maps, charts) of performance
Identify metrics outside target range for discussion
controls.riskAddressed: Leadership lacks visibility of barrier health; performance trends not detected; management decisions made without complete information
controls.testingApproach: Verify data collected from authoritative sources; confirm all required indicators included; review compilation for accuracy; validate trends correctly calculated

controls.controlName: Conduct Asset-Level Annual AIPSM Effectiveness Review
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Leadership Team, technical/operations/process safety TA2, HSSE Lead | What: Conduct comprehensive annual review of asset integrity to verify process safety risks managed to ALARP and identify improvement actions | When: Annually as part of asset management cycle | Where: Management review meeting, documented review report | Why: To systematically assess AIPSM program effectiveness and identify systemic improvements needed | How: Review process safety leading/lagging indicators and trends; assess barrier health status; review risk action management; examine incident investigations; assess technical integrity of key equipment; review competency status; identify improvement themes and priorities; document findings and actions | Evidence: Documented AIPSM review report with findings and action items, management team attendance records, tracked closure of action items
keyActivities: Review all process safety leading and lagging indicators
Assess barrier health across design, technical, operational, and leadership aspects
Review risk-related action management from various sources
Examine top process safety scenarios and control status
Identify improvement actions and assign accountability
Track action closure through management system
controls.riskAddressed: AIPSM program effectiveness not assessed; systemic weaknesses not identified; improvement opportunities missed; management unaware of changing risk profile
controls.testingApproach: Verify review conducted with appropriate team; review documented findings; confirm action items assigned with clear accountability; check tracking system for action closure

controls.controlName: Identify Events Requiring Statement of Fitness Review
controls.controlStatement: Who: Production Unit Manager, Operations Supervisor, HSSE Lead | What: Identify when significant abnormal events occur requiring SoF review (uncontrolled shutdowns, turnarounds, equipment constraint exceedances, environmental incidents) | When: Immediately when abnormal event occurs or is anticipated | Where: Event log, management communications | Why: To ensure structured technical review conducted before facility restart; to verify controls remain effective after disruption | How: Establish clear SoF trigger criteria in local procedure; train personnel on recognition; document event identification and SoF decision | Evidence: Event notification, documented identification of SoF trigger event, decision record
keyActivities: Define SoF trigger criteria in local procedure
Train personnel on event recognition and reporting
Establish communication process for event notification
Document event identification and SoF decision
Notify technical team and management
controls.riskAddressed: Abnormal events not recognized as SoF triggers; facility restarted without assessment; previously valid controls compromised undetected
controls.testingApproach: Verify local SoF procedure exists with clear trigger criteria; confirm training records show personnel trained; review event logs for proper identification and documentation

controls.controlName: Conduct Structured Statement of Fitness Review and Document Findings
controls.controlStatement: Who: Multi-disciplined team (Operations, Process Engineering, TA2, HSSE), led by SoF Review Leader | What: Conduct comprehensive technical review of facility following abnormal event; assess whether controls remain adequate to manage risks to ALARP; identify needed repairs or interim measures | When: Prior to facility restart | Where: SoF review meeting, SoF form, supporting documentation | Why: To provide documented assurance process safety risks adequately managed before resuming operations | How: Investigate event root cause; identify equipment damage or parameter exceedances; assess impact on barriers and controls; conduct risk assessment; define needed repairs/modifications; verify procedures and training; complete SoF form with multi-disciplinary signatures | Evidence: Completed SoF form with all reviews documented, incident investigation report, risk assessment documentation, repair/modification work orders, training and procedure updates
keyActivities: Conduct root cause investigation of abnormal event
Assess equipment and system damage or degradation
Review impact on process safety barriers and controls
Conduct risk assessment for identified impacts
Define required repairs, modifications, or interim measures
Verify procedures and training updated for restart
Obtain multi-disciplinary team approval
controls.riskAddressed: Facility restarted with inadequate controls after upset; hidden damage not identified; barriers compromised; new risks introduced during recovery
controls.testingApproach: Review SoF form for completeness of all required sections; verify incident investigation documented; confirm risk assessment completed; check repairs/modifications verified or tracked

controls.controlName: Identify and Rate Hazards Using Risk Assessment Matrix
controls.controlStatement: Who: Hazard identification team, Process Safety SME | What: Identify all hazards and rate residual risk using Shell HSSE & SP Risk Assessment Matrix; document in Hazards and Effects Register | When: During initial hazard identification and updated through MOC and learning processes | Where: Hazards and Effects Register (HER), RAM documentation | Why: To establish baseline understanding of risk magnitude; to determine level of ALARP documentation effort required | How: Systematically identify hazards; assess severity and probability of consequences; apply RAM to obtain rating; document rating rationale; update HER with current ratings | Evidence: Complete HER with all hazards and risk ratings documented, RAM application documentation showing assessment methodology
keyActivities: Conduct systematic hazard identification
Assess severity of potential consequences
Assess probability of occurrence
Apply RAM to determine risk rating
Document rating rationale in HER
Update HER with current assessments
controls.riskAddressed: Hazards not identified; risk ratings incorrect; inappropriate control effort allocated; ALARP documentation insufficient for risk magnitude
controls.testingApproach: Review HER for completeness of hazard identification; verify RAM ratings correctly assigned; confirm rating rationale documented; check HER updated with current information

controls.controlName: Document ALARP Demonstration for RED and YELLOW 5A/5B Scenarios
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Safety TA2, Engineering Discipline Leads, Risk Assessment Team | What: Prepare comprehensive documented justification that RED and YELLOW 5A/5B risks managed to ALARP; explain standards applied, alternatives considered, and barriers implemented | When: Following hazard analysis; reviewed periodically or when significant changes occur | Where: ALARP Demonstration document, project HSSE Case or asset safety documentation | Why: To provide regulatory and business justification that all reasonable measures taken to reduce risk; to satisfy legal obligations for major accident hazard management | How: Identify applicable standards and good practices; document how scenario addresses standards; describe alternatives considered and reason for rejection; explain barriers selected and reliability basis; document cost-benefit analysis where applicable; obtain technical approval | Evidence: Comprehensive ALARP Demonstration document covering each RED/YELLOW scenario, cited standards and practices, cost-benefit analysis, technical approval signatures
keyActivities: Identify applicable standards and good practices for each scenario
Document how scenario controls address standards
List and evaluate alternatives considered
Explain barrier selection rationale
Perform cost-benefit analysis for major controls
Obtain technical authority approval
controls.riskAddressed: ALARP cannot be justified; regulatory non-compliance; gaps in risk reduction logic; inadequate cost-benefit analysis; controls not substantiated
controls.testingApproach: Review ALARP documentation for each RED/YELLOW scenario; verify standards cited are applicable; confirm alternatives discussion includes rejection rationale; validate cost-benefit analysis performed

controls.controlName: Establish Management of Change Process for Design and Operational Changes
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager, HSSE Lead, Technical Authority | What: Establish formal MOC process requiring assessment of safety and environmental impacts before any design changes, operational changes, or facility modifications are implemented | When: Integrated into asset management system from startup and continuously maintained | Where: MOC procedure documentation, MOC evaluation form, change request system | Why: To ensure changes do not introduce new hazards, degrade barriers, or create non-compliance; to maintain control and documentation of changes | How: Develop MOC procedure defining change categories (design, operational, temporary modifications); require technical safety assessment for all changes; document change details, reasons, impacts, and safeguards; obtain necessary approvals; execute change per approved plan; verify post-change system operates as designed | Evidence: Documented MOC procedure with approval authority levels, completed MOC evaluation forms, change implementation records, post-change verification documentation
keyActivities: Develop comprehensive MOC procedure with defined change categories
Require technical safety assessment for all changes
Document change details, reasons, and proposed safeguards
Obtain appropriate approvals based on change significance
Execute change per approved plan with monitoring
Verify post-change system operation matches approved design
controls.riskAddressed: Changes implemented without safety assessment; new hazards introduced; barriers degraded; system operates differently than intended; non-compliance from unauthorized changes
controls.testingApproach: Verify MOC procedure in place and implemented; review recent MOC evaluation forms for completeness; confirm approvals obtained for significant changes; check post-change verification completed

controls.controlName: Conduct Technical Review and Approval of Proposed Changes
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Safety TA2, Discipline Engineers, Process Engineer | What: Conduct technical review of each MOC evaluating proposed change against design standards, process safety requirements, and equipment capabilities; approve, approve with conditions, or reject change | When: Prior to change implementation | Where: MOC review meeting, MOC evaluation documentation | Why: To ensure proposed change is technically sound and does not compromise safety or compliance | How: Review MOC evaluation package; assess technical feasibility; evaluate safety and environmental impacts; identify any additional safeguards needed; provide approval or recommend modifications; document review findings and approval | Evidence: Completed MOC evaluation form with technical review documented, approvals/rejections with justification, any required additional safeguards specified
keyActivities: Review proposed change technical feasibility
Assess design standards and requirements compliance
Evaluate safety and environmental impacts
Identify any additional safeguards or testing needed
Provide approval with justification or recommend modifications
controls.riskAddressed: Technically unsound changes approved; safety impacts not assessed; inadequate safeguards identified; compliance issues not caught before implementation
controls.testingApproach: Review MOC evaluation for technical assessment completeness; verify TA2/discipline engineer reviewed and approved; confirm safety impacts assessed; check additional safeguards specified if needed

controls.controlName: Establish Risk Assessment Process and Identify All HSSE and SP Risks
controls.controlStatement: Who: Risk Assessment Team, HSSE Lead, Subject Matter Experts | What: Conduct comprehensive identification of HSSE and SP risks through hazard identification, consequence assessment, and risk rating; establish risk register documenting all identified risks | When: During asset planning and periodically updated through assurance and learning processes | Where: Risk assessment documentation, Risk Register | Why: To ensure all significant risks identified and have management attention proportional to risk magnitude | How: Conduct systematic hazard identification workshops; assess consequences and probability; rate risks using established matrix; document risk details; establish risk ownership; track risk treatment status; update risk register periodically | Evidence: Documented risk assessment results, complete Risk Register with all identified risks, risk treatment status tracking, management review records
keyActivities: Conduct systematic HSSE and SP hazard identification
Assess consequence severity and probability
Apply risk rating matrix and rate all identified risks
Establish Risk Register documenting all risks
Assign risk owner for each identified risk
Track risk treatment status and effectiveness
controls.riskAddressed: Significant risks not identified; risk register incomplete; management attention not proportional to risk magnitude; risks not actively managed
controls.testingApproach: Review risk assessment documentation for completeness; verify all significant risks identified and in Risk Register; confirm risk ratings documented with rationale; check that risks have assigned owners

controls.controlName: Develop and Implement Risk Treatment Plans for Identified Risks
controls.controlStatement: Who: Risk Owner, Risk Assessment Team, Technical Authority | What: For each identified risk, develop risk treatment plan addressing the risk through engineering controls, administrative controls, or risk acceptance decision; implement approved treatment plans | When: Following risk identification; implemented per agreed timeline | Where: Risk treatment plans, control implementation documentation | Why: To systematically reduce risks to As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP) and prevent escalation | How: For each risk above acceptable threshold, develop treatment plan addressing root cause; implement engineering controls where feasible, supplemented by administrative controls; verify control implementation; track treatment effectiveness; for residual risks, document risk acceptance by management | Evidence: Documented risk treatment plans for each unacceptable risk, control implementation records, verification that controls functioning as designed, risk acceptance documentation where applicable
keyActivities: Develop risk treatment plan for each unacceptable risk
Prioritize engineering controls, supported by administrative controls
Implement approved risk treatment plans per timeline
Verify control implementation and effectiveness
Document risk acceptance for residual risks
Track risk treatment status to completion
controls.riskAddressed: Identified risks not addressed; controls not implemented; risks remain unmanaged; residual risk not formally accepted; control effectiveness not verified
controls.testingApproach: Review risk treatment plans for identified unacceptable risks; verify controls implemented and functioning; confirm risk acceptance documented for residual risks; check treatment tracking system

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Establish Design Operating Window (DOW) Parameters
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Engineer, Technical Authority, Design Team | What: Define Design Operating Window (DOW) specifying acceptable operating ranges for critical process parameters based on equipment design limits and safety requirements | When: During asset design/HAZOP and reviewed when equipment changes occur | Where: Process design documentation, operating procedures | Why: To provide clear operational boundaries ensuring equipment operates within design intent; to prevent equipment stress, failure, or safety incidents | How: Assess equipment design specifications and limits; define safe operating ranges for all critical parameters (temperatures, pressures, rates, levels); include safety margins; document DOW with technical basis; communicate DOW to operations; identify consequences of exceeding limits | Evidence: Documented DOW specification with technical basis, equipment specification references, operating procedures reflecting DOW parameters
keyActivities: Review equipment design specifications and limits
Assess failure modes if limits exceeded
Define safe operating ranges for critical parameters
Include appropriate safety margins
Document DOW with technical basis
Communicate DOW to operations personnel
controls.riskAddressed: Operations proceed outside safe limits; equipment stressed beyond design; unplanned shutdowns; barrier failure from equipment degradation
controls.testingApproach: Verify DOW documented with equipment specification basis; check procedures reflect DOW parameters; observe operator knowledge of limits; confirm communication of consequences

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Monitor Operations Within Design Operating Window and Manage Exceedances
controls.controlStatement: Who: Operations Supervisor, Console Operator, Process Engineer | What: Continuously monitor process parameters against DOW limits; immediately respond to approaches to limits; investigate and document any exceedances; assess equipment impact and required actions | When: Continuously throughout operations | Where: Control system displays, alarm system, exceedance investigation records | Why: To detect and respond to out-of-limit conditions before equipment damage occurs; to maintain asset integrity | How: Configure process control system with alarm setpoints reflecting DOW limits; establish escalation procedure for alarm triggers; train operators on limit significance and response; investigate all exceedances; assess impact on equipment integrity; document findings and corrective actions | Evidence: Alarm setpoint documentation, exceedance investigation records, equipment condition assessment following exceedance, corrective action documentation
keyActivities: Configure alarm setpoints at DOW limits with appropriate warning margin
Establish operator response procedure for alarms
Train operators on DOW significance and response requirements
Investigate all DOW exceedances
Assess equipment impact and required repairs
Document exceedance and corrective actions
controls.riskAddressed: DOW exceeded without detection; equipment damage occurs; barrier integrity compromised; unplanned shutdown results from inadequate monitoring
controls.testingApproach: Verify alarm setpoints configured at DOW limits; observe operator response to simulated limit approach; review exceedance investigation reports; confirm equipment assessment conducted

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.subProcess: Interim Operating Window (IOW) and Safeguarding Override
controls.controlName: Manage Safeguarding Overrides and Interim Operating Windows
controls.controlStatement: Who: Operations Manager, Technical Authority, HSSE Lead | What: When equipment degradation or temporary conditions require operation at reduced limits (Interim Operating Window), establish compensating controls; manage any safeguarding system overrides with documented justification and action plans | When: When IOW needed due to equipment status or when override required for maintenance/testing | Where: IOW documentation, safeguarding override records, compensating controls implementation | Why: To allow continued operations safely when full DOW not available; to maintain control of risks during override conditions | How: Assess equipment condition and establish temporary IOW with reduced safe limits; implement enhanced monitoring and additional safeguards to compensate for degraded equipment; document IOW basis and expiration date; any safeguarding system override requires documented justification, compensating control plan, and reinstatement verification; obtain management approval for override | Evidence: Documented IOW specification with technical basis, compensating controls implemented and verified, safeguarding override approval record, reinstatement verification
keyActivities: Assess equipment condition and establish IOW limits
Identify necessary compensating controls
Implement and verify compensating controls
Document IOW basis and expiration timeline
For safeguarding overrides, obtain management approval
Verify safeguarding system reinstatement
controls.riskAddressed: IOW implemented without compensating controls; residual risk unmanaged; safeguarding override continues indefinitely; equipment failure with override in place
controls.testingApproach: Verify IOW documented with technical basis; confirm compensating controls implemented; check that override has time limit and reinstatement plan; observe safeguarding system functionality

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Establish Process Safety Barrier Verification Program
controls.controlStatement: Who: Barrier Owner, Barrier Engineer, Technical Authority | What: Establish program to verify that process safety barriers remain at required reliability through inspection, testing, maintenance, and assessment; document barrier status and required actions | When: Per barrier-specific verification schedule (typically 1-5 years depending on barrier type) | Where: Barrier verification plan, test records, barrier status documentation | Why: To ensure barriers function as designed and maintain required reliability; to detect degradation before failure | How: For each HSSE Critical Barrier, establish verification method appropriate to barrier type (physical inspection, functional testing, integrity assessment); define verification frequency; execute verifications per schedule; document results and any deficiencies found; perform corrective maintenance if needed; verify barrier restored to required condition | Evidence: Barrier verification plan with schedule, completed verification records with test results, corrective action records for any deficiencies found, barrier status summary
keyActivities: Identify all HSSE Critical Process Safety Barriers
Establish verification method appropriate to barrier type
Define verification frequency based on barrier criticality
Execute planned verifications per schedule
Document verification results and findings
Perform corrective actions for any deficiencies
controls.riskAddressed: Barrier degradation undetected; barrier fails to function when demanded; reliability assumption invalid; control effectiveness not verified
controls.testingApproach: Review barrier verification plan for completeness; verify planned verifications conducted on schedule; review test results and any deficiencies documented; confirm corrective actions completed

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Investigate Barrier Failures and Implement Preventive Actions
controls.controlStatement: Who: Barrier Owner, Investigation Team, Technical Authority | What: When HSSE Critical Barrier fails to function as designed, conduct investigation to identify root cause and implement preventive actions preventing recurrence | When: Immediately upon discovery of barrier failure | Where: Barrier failure investigation documentation, corrective action records | Why: To understand why barrier failed; to prevent similar failures at same facility or other assets; to maintain barrier reliability | How: Upon discovery of failure, preserve evidence; assess consequences of failure; investigate root cause (design deficiency, material degradation, operational error, maintenance deficiency); develop preventive action addressing root cause; implement corrective actions; verify barrier now functions reliably; communicate findings and lessons learned | Evidence: Completed barrier failure investigation report with root cause analysis, identified preventive actions, implementation records, barrier functionality verification
keyActivities: Immediately isolate and assess barrier failure
Preserve evidence and document failure mode
Assess consequences and impact of failure
Conduct root cause investigation
Develop preventive actions addressing root cause
Implement and verify corrective actions
controls.riskAddressed: Barrier failure cause not understood; similar failures occur; barrier remains non-functional; organizational learning lost
controls.testingApproach: Verify barrier failure investigation conducted; review root cause analysis for completeness; confirm preventive actions address identified causes; check barrier restoration to functional status

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Assess Equipment Degradation and Manage Barrier Reliability Impact
controls.controlStatement: Who: Equipment Engineer, Asset Manager, Technical Authority | What: When HSSE Critical Equipment shows signs of degradation, assess impact on barrier reliability; implement compensating controls if necessary; plan equipment restoration or replacement | When: Upon detection of equipment degradation; assessed at least annually for critical equipment | Where: Equipment condition assessment records, barrier impact assessment, maintenance plan | Why: To prevent loss of barrier function due to equipment degradation; to maintain asset integrity | How: Conduct condition assessment of critical equipment; evaluate impact on any process safety barriers the equipment supports; if impact significant, implement compensating controls; plan equipment restoration or replacement; track equipment status and corrective action plan execution | Evidence: Equipment condition assessment reports, barrier impact assessment documentation, compensating controls implemented if necessary, equipment restoration/replacement plan
keyActivities: Conduct condition assessment of HSSE Critical Equipment
Evaluate impact on supported process safety barriers
Identify compensating controls if barrier impact significant
Implement compensating controls and verify effectiveness
Develop equipment restoration or replacement plan
Track plan execution and barrier restoration
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment degradation reduces barrier reliability undetected; barrier function lost; residual risk unmanaged; compensating controls not implemented
controls.testingApproach: Review equipment condition assessment for critical equipment; verify barrier impact assessed; confirm compensating controls implemented if needed; check restoration/replacement plan and status

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Conduct First and Second Line Assurance of Barrier Performance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Frontline Supervisors (First Line), Technical Auditors (Second Line) | What: Conduct periodic assurance activities verifying that HSSE Critical Barriers are functioning as designed and required controls are being executed; document findings and corrective actions | When: First line: continuously through supervision; Second line: minimum biennial independent audit | Where: Assurance observation records, audit documentation | Why: To provide systematic verification that barriers functioning and controls executed; to identify degradation or non-compliance early | How: First line: conduct observations of barrier function and control execution; Second line: conduct independent audit evaluating barrier reliability, control execution, maintenance status, and compliance; document findings with evidence; identify deficiencies; track corrective actions to closure | Evidence: First line assurance records (observation logs), Second line audit reports with findings, corrective action tracking
keyActivities: Conduct first line observations of barrier function
Observe critical control execution during operations
Document observations and any non-conformances
Conduct second line independent audits biennial minimum
Evaluate barrier reliability and control effectiveness
Track corrective actions identified in audits
controls.riskAddressed: Barrier degradation not detected by operations; control execution drifts from procedure; compliance issues not identified; assurance gaps exist
controls.testingApproach: Verify first line assurance activities conducted; review observation records for completeness; confirm second line audit conducted at required frequency; review audit findings and corrective action tracking

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Obtain Technical Authority Assurance on Barrier Reliability and Design
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Authority (TA2), Design Engineer | What: Conduct periodic technical review of design basis, barrier reliability assumptions, and maintenance strategy for HSSE Critical Barriers and equipment; provide assurance that design and strategy remain appropriate | When: At asset startup and reviewed biennial or when design changes occur | Where: Technical review documentation, barrier reliability assessment reports | Why: To provide technical confidence in design integrity and maintenance effectiveness; to identify design-basis changes requiring strategy updates | How: Review design specifications and design basis documents; assess barrier reliability claims against field performance; evaluate maintenance strategy effectiveness; assess impact of any design changes or learning; recommend improvements if needed; document review findings and any required actions | Evidence: Completed technical review documentation, design basis confirmation or recommended changes, barrier reliability assessment, maintenance strategy review
keyActivities: Review design basis documents and assumptions
Assess barrier reliability claims against field performance
Evaluate maintenance strategy effectiveness
Assess impact of design changes or operational learning
Identify improvements or changes needed
Document findings and recommendations
controls.riskAddressed: Design basis no longer valid; reliability assumptions not substantiated; maintenance strategy ineffective; design changes not reflected in barrier management
controls.testingApproach: Verify technical review conducted at required frequency; review design basis documentation; confirm barrier reliability assessment completed; check that maintenance strategy reflects field performance

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Maintain Process Safety Documentation and Regulatory Compliance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager, HEMP Focal Point, HSSE Lead | What: Maintain comprehensive, current documentation of process safety program including design basis, hazard analysis, ALARP justification, barrier specifications, maintenance plans, and verification records; ensure compliance with applicable regulations | When: Continuously throughout asset lifecycle | Where: Documentation repository, management system | Why: To provide regulatory authorities and internal stakeholders with evidence of process safety management; to enable effective assurance and learning | How: Establish documentation library with design basis, HEMP, barrier specifications, maintenance procedures, verification records, incident investigations, and assurance results; keep documentation current per change management; conduct periodic compliance audits against applicable regulations; maintain records per regulatory requirements | Evidence: Complete process safety documentation library, documented compliance verification, audit reports showing regulatory compliance
keyActivities: Establish comprehensive process safety documentation library
Keep HEMP and design basis documents current
Maintain barrier specifications and verification records
Document all process safety changes through MOC
Conduct periodic regulatory compliance audits
Maintain records per applicable regulatory timeframes
controls.riskAddressed: Documentation incomplete or out-of-date; regulatory compliance not verified; assurance bodies lack evidence; learning from incidents not captured
controls.testingApproach: Verify process safety documentation library exists and is organized; confirm HEMP current; check that design basis documents accessible; verify regulatory compliance audit conducted

controls.processArea: Integrity and Design Operating Window Management
controls.controlName: Apply Risk-Based Approach to Barrier Prioritization and Resource Allocation
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager, Technical Authority, HEMP Coordinator | What: Prioritize process safety management effort and resources based on magnitude of risk addressed by each barrier; focus highest effort on barriers controlling highest-consequence scenarios | When: During asset planning and periodically reviewed per assurance cycle | Where: Risk Register, barrier prioritization matrix, resource allocation plan | Why: To ensure management effort and resources appropriately focused on highest-risk areas; to optimize safety management effectiveness | How: Map each HSSE Critical Barrier to managed hazard scenarios; assess risk magnitude of each scenario; rank scenarios/barriers by risk; allocate management and verification resources proportional to risk magnitude; review allocation periodically and adjust as risks change | Evidence: Barrier-to-scenario mapping, risk-based prioritization matrix, resource allocation plan showing effort proportional to barrier risk contribution
keyActivities: Map each barrier to managed hazard scenarios
Assess consequence severity of each scenario
Rank scenarios/barriers by risk magnitude
Allocate management effort proportional to risk
Focus verification frequency on highest-risk barriers
Review and adjust allocation as risks change
controls.riskAddressed: Management effort not proportional to risk magnitude; low-risk barriers over-managed; high-risk barriers under-resourced; inefficient resource allocation
controls.testingApproach: Review barrier-to-scenario mapping; verify high-risk barriers receive appropriate management attention; check that resource allocation proportional to risk; confirm prioritization updated with risk changes

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Asset Care Plan
controls.controlName: Develop Equipment Care Strategy Based on Criticality and Reliability
controls.controlStatement: Who: Equipment Engineer, Maintenance Planner, Reliability SME | What: Develop maintenance strategy for each equipment item based on criticality to production and process safety, failure modes, and reliability requirements | When: During equipment specification/selection; updated per reliability reviews | Where: Equipment Care Strategy documentation, maintenance plans | Why: To ensure maintenance effort appropriately focused on critical equipment and high-failure-risk items | How: Assess equipment criticality (impact of failure on production, safety, environment); identify failure modes and consequences; select appropriate maintenance strategy (run-to-failure, fixed-interval PM, condition-based); document strategy rationale; ensure strategy resources available | Evidence: Documented Equipment Care Strategy for critical equipment, maintenance strategy selection rationale, allocated resources, strategy implementation plan
keyActivities: Assess equipment criticality to production and safety
Identify failure modes and consequences
Evaluate failure risk magnitude
Select appropriate maintenance strategy
Document strategy rationale and assumptions
Identify required resources
controls.riskAddressed: Maintenance effort not focused on critical equipment; low-risk equipment over-maintained; critical equipment under-maintained; maintenance strategy inappropriate for risk
controls.testingApproach: Review equipment care strategies for appropriateness to criticality; verify resources allocated; check that critical equipment has proactive maintenance approach

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Asset Care Plan
controls.controlName: Execute Asset Care Plan and Monitor Performance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Maintenance Technician, Maintenance Planner, Operations Manager | What: Execute planned maintenance activities per developed strategy; track completion; monitor equipment performance and condition to verify strategy effectiveness | When: Continuously throughout asset lifecycle | Where: Maintenance system, work orders, equipment performance data | Why: To maintain equipment in operational condition; to deliver promised reliability and availability | How: Schedule maintenance per strategy; execute work per procedures; track completion and findings; collect performance data (uptime, failure rate, repair frequency); compare actual performance against strategy targets; adjust strategy if performance does not meet expectations | Evidence: Completed maintenance work orders, equipment performance data showing uptime and reliability metrics, maintenance completion rates tracking against schedule, strategy effectiveness assessment
keyActivities: Schedule maintenance per strategy
Execute maintenance work orders per procedure
Collect equipment performance data
Track maintenance completion
Monitor equipment uptime and reliability
Compare performance to strategy targets
controls.riskAddressed: Maintenance not executed per strategy; equipment performance deteriorates; reliability targets not achieved; unplanned failures occur
controls.testingApproach: Verify maintenance scheduled and executed per plan; review equipment performance data; compare actual performance to strategy targets; confirm adjustments made if performance inadequate

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Pressure Equipment Integrity
controls.controlName: Establish Pressure Equipment Integrity Management System
