controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager, Pressure Equipment Engineering Lead, HSSE Lead | What: Establish management system for all pressure-containing equipment addressing design compliance, operating limits, inspection, maintenance, and leak management | When: During asset design and maintained throughout lifecycle | Where: Pressure Equipment Integrity Management (PEIM) procedure, equipment inventory, maintenance plans | Why: To ensure pressure equipment operates safely within design limits and potential releases managed | How: Identify all pressure-containing equipment; establish design compliance verification; document operating limits; establish proactive inspection and leak reporting process; develop maintenance procedures; establish process for equipment modification or repair; train personnel on pressure safety hazards | Evidence: Documented PEIM procedure, complete pressure equipment inventory with design specifications, operating limit documentation, maintenance schedules, leak reporting system
keyActivities: Identify all pressure-containing equipment
Verify design compliance with standards
Document operating limits for each equipment
Establish maintenance inspection schedule
Develop leak reporting and investigation process
Establish modification/repair approval process
controls.riskAddressed: Pressure equipment operating beyond design limits; design non-compliance undetected; leaks not detected; maintenance inadequate; potential release uncontrolled
controls.testingApproach: Verify PEIM procedure established and implemented; check pressure equipment inventory current; confirm operating limits documented; review maintenance schedules; verify leak reporting system in use

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Pressure Equipment Integrity
controls.controlName: Conduct Periodic Inspection and Proof Testing of Pressure Equipment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Qualified Pressure Equipment Inspector, Equipment Engineer | What: Conduct visual inspections and periodic proof/hydrostatic testing of pressure-containing equipment to verify integrity and detect degradation before failure | When: Per risk-based inspection schedule (typically 5-10 yearly depending on equipment type and service history) | Where: Equipment location, inspection records, test certification | Why: To detect wall thinning, corrosion, cracking, or other degradation before equipment failure; to verify integrity after service | How: Conduct visual inspection per industry standards; perform proof testing per manufacturer recommendations and regulatory requirements; document test parameters and results; assess findings against acceptance criteria; perform corrective action if defects found; issue inspection certification when passed | Evidence: Completed inspection reports with measurements and observations, test certificates with pressure applied and duration, any defects found and corrective actions taken, equipment recertification documentation
keyActivities: Conduct visual inspection per industry standards
Measure critical dimensions and wall thickness
Perform proof/hydrostatic test per requirements
Document test parameters and results
Assess findings against acceptance criteria
Perform corrective actions if defects found
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment degradation undetected; equipment failure occurs without warning; defects not identified before potential release; integrity assumptions invalid
controls.testingApproach: Verify inspections conducted at required frequency; review inspection reports for completeness; check test certificates for proper pressure and duration; confirm defects identified and corrected

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Electrical Equipment Integrity
controls.controlName: Establish Electrical Equipment Integrity Management Program
controls.controlStatement: Who: Electrical Engineering Lead, Maintenance Manager, HSSE Lead | What: Establish program managing electrical equipment safety including asset inventory, design compliance verification, maintenance strategies, and testing requirements | When: During asset design and maintained throughout lifecycle | Where: Electrical Equipment Integrity procedure, equipment inventory, maintenance specifications | Why: To prevent electrical hazards (shock, arc flash, fire) and ensure electrical power/control systems remain functional | How: Identify all electrical equipment and systems; verify design compliance with electrical standards; establish risk-based maintenance strategy by equipment type (motors, cables, switchgear, protective devices); develop testing procedures; establish procedure for equipment modifications; train personnel on electrical hazards | Evidence: Documented electrical equipment management procedure, complete equipment inventory with design verification, maintenance specifications for each equipment type, electrical safety training records
keyActivities: Identify all electrical equipment and systems
Verify design compliance with electrical standards
Establish risk-based maintenance strategy
Develop testing and maintenance specifications
Establish modification approval process
Conduct electrical safety training
controls.riskAddressed: Electrical hazards (shock, arc flash, fire) unmanaged; equipment failure leads to process upset; maintenance inadequate; design non-compliance undetected
controls.testingApproach: Verify electrical equipment management procedure established; check inventory completeness; confirm design verification documented; review maintenance specifications for appropriateness

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Reliability Centered Maintenance
controls.controlName: Conduct Reliability Centered Maintenance Analysis for Critical Assets
controls.controlStatement: Who: RCM Facilitator, Equipment Engineer, Maintenance Manager | What: Conduct systematic RCM analysis for critical equipment using structured methodology to identify optimal maintenance strategy based on failure modes, consequences, and technical feasibility | When: For new critical equipment at design stage; for existing critical equipment as part of maintenance optimization | Where: RCM analysis reports, updated maintenance procedures | Why: To optimize maintenance strategies ensuring required reliability while managing maintenance costs | How: Assemble cross-functional team including operations, engineering, maintenance; conduct failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA) for critical equipment; identify failure consequences; evaluate maintenance task options (condition monitoring, preventive maintenance, design improvement, no planned maintenance); select maintenance strategy providing required reliability with reasonable cost and effort; implement selected strategy | Evidence: Documented RCM analysis reports with FMEA, evaluated maintenance task options, selected maintenance strategy with technical justification, updated maintenance procedures reflecting RCM findings
keyActivities: Assemble RCM analysis team with cross-functional expertise
Conduct failure mode and effects analysis for equipment
Identify failure consequences and required reliability
Evaluate maintenance task options for each failure mode
Select maintenance strategy providing required reliability
Implement and monitor effectiveness of selected strategy
controls.riskAddressed: Maintenance strategy not optimized; unnecessary maintenance performed; critical failures not prevented; maintenance costs excessive or unreliable
controls.testingApproach: Review RCM analysis for completeness of failure mode identification; verify maintenance strategy selection justified by analysis; confirm strategy implemented in maintenance system; monitor equipment reliability against RCM targets

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Reliability Centered Maintenance
controls.controlName: Implement Condition Monitoring for Early Detection of Equipment Degradation
controls.controlStatement: Who: Condition Monitoring Technician, Equipment Engineer | What: Implement condition monitoring program for critical equipment using appropriate techniques (vibration, temperature, oil analysis, ultrasound) to detect early signs of degradation | When: Continuously for critical equipment on condition monitoring program | Where: Condition monitoring procedures, trending data, equipment condition records | Why: To provide early warning of equipment degradation enabling preventive action before failure; to optimize preventive maintenance intervals | How: Select appropriate monitoring technique for each equipment type based on failure modes; establish baseline condition; establish monitoring frequency and acceptance criteria; collect data on schedule; analyze trends; alert maintenance when trending toward failure; schedule preventive action before failure occurs | Evidence: Condition monitoring procedures by equipment type, baseline condition documentation, trending data showing early degradation detection, preventive actions scheduled based on condition monitoring results
keyActivities: Select appropriate condition monitoring technique by equipment type
Establish baseline condition for trending reference
Define monitoring frequency and acceptance criteria
Collect condition data on schedule
Analyze trends and identify degradation patterns
Schedule preventive action when degradation detected
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment degradation undetected until failure occurs; preventive maintenance opportunity missed; unplanned equipment failure and downtime
controls.testingApproach: Verify condition monitoring procedures documented; check baseline condition established; review trending data for early degradation detection; confirm preventive actions scheduled based on monitoring results

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Safety Instrumented Systems
controls.controlName: Design Safety Instrumented Systems to Achieve Required Safety Integrity Level
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Safety Engineer, SIS Designer, Technical Authority | What: Design Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) addressing identified hazards with architecture, component selection, and testing strategy to achieve required Safety Integrity Level (SIL) | When: During HAZOP/risk assessment and design phase | Where: SIS design specification, safety requirement specification, design documentation | Why: To ensure SIS provides required hazard mitigation with appropriate reliability and integrity | How: Identify SIS functions required from hazard analysis; determine required SIL for each function; select SIS architecture meeting SIL requirements; select components with appropriate certified SIL capability; design proof testing strategy; document design basis and justification; perform design review and technical approval | Evidence: Documented SIS design specification including required SIL for each function, component selection with SIL certification, design basis documentation, design review and approval records
keyActivities: Identify SIS functions required from hazard analysis
Determine required Safety Integrity Level for each function
Select SIS architecture meeting SIL requirements
Select components with certified SIL capability
Design proof testing strategy
Document design basis with technical justification
controls.riskAddressed: SIS design inadequate for required SIL; selected architecture/components insufficient; testing strategy inadequate; SIS fails to achieve required safety function
controls.testingApproach: Review SIS design specification for SIL requirement justification; verify components selected with SIL certification; check design review conducted; confirm proof testing strategy documented

controls.processArea: Managed Asset Care - Safety Instrumented Systems
controls.controlName: Conduct SIS Proof Testing and Maintain SIS Functionality
controls.controlStatement: Who: SIS Test Technician, Instrument Technician, Operations Supervisor | What: Conduct periodic proof testing of SIS components and logic to verify functionality and measure actual Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD); maintain SIS in functional condition between proof tests | When: Per proof test interval (typically 1-5 years depending on component type and risk); maintenance as needed for any discovered deficiencies | Where: SIS testing procedures, proof test records, maintenance work orders | Why: To verify SIS functions correctly when demanded; to measure and maintain reliability at required level; to provide data for SIL calculations | How: Develop proof test procedures for each SIS function; conduct proof testing per procedure documenting results; calculate actual PFD from test data; compare actual PFD to required level; if below required level, investigate cause and implement corrective action; maintain SIS between proof tests per maintenance program | Evidence: Documented proof test procedures, completed proof test records with measured PFD, calculation of actual SIL demonstrated, SIS maintenance records
keyActivities: Develop proof test procedure for each SIS function
Execute proof testing per documented procedure
Document test results and measured PFD
Calculate actual SIL from measured PFD
Compare actual SIL to required level
Implement corrective actions if SIL inadequate
controls.riskAddressed: SIS fails when demanded; proof testing inadequate; actual SIL less than required; SIS defects not detected; maintenance inadequate for functionality
controls.testingApproach: Verify proof test procedures documented and current; review proof test records for completeness and proper documentation; confirm measured PFD within acceptable range; check that corrective actions implemented for any deficiencies

controls.controlName: Assess Equipment Reliability and Identify Chronic Failure Issues
controls.controlStatement: Who: Reliability Engineer, Maintenance Manager, Equipment Engineer | What: Analyze equipment failure data and maintenance history to identify chronically unreliable equipment ('bad actors'); assess failure modes and contributing causes | When: Periodically (at least annually) as part of maintenance optimization; when equipment shows patterns of failure | Where: Reliability analysis reports, failure trend documentation | Why: To identify equipment requiring enhanced attention; to prioritize reliability improvement efforts | How: Compile failure history and maintenance records; analyze failure frequency and patterns; calculate Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF); identify repeat failure modes; assess root causes (design, maintenance, operation); rank equipment by reliability risk | Evidence: Reliability analysis documentation showing failure trends, identified 'bad actor' equipment, failure mode analysis, improvement prioritization
keyActivities: Compile failure and maintenance data for equipment
Analyze failure frequency and patterns
Calculate reliability metrics (MTBF, MTTR)
Identify repeat failure modes
Assess root causes of failures
Rank equipment by reliability risk
controls.riskAddressed: Chronically unreliable equipment continues with high failure rate; unplanned downtime persists; barrier reliability degraded; opportunity for improvement missed
controls.testingApproach: Review reliability analysis for completeness; verify failure data analyzed; confirm bad actor equipment identified; check improvement recommendations documented

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Ensure Plan Delivery
controls.controlName: Develop Production Plan Within Design Operating Window Parameters
controls.controlStatement: Who: Production Planning Team, Process Engineer, Operations Manager | What: Develop production plan (rates, product slate, campaign duration) within approved Design Operating Window (DOW) parameters; obtain management approval | When: Periodically as part of production scheduling; updated for significant changes | Where: Production plan document, approved by management | Why: To ensure production operations remain within equipment design limits; to prevent equipment stress and unplanned shutdowns | How: Review current DOW parameters and limitations; develop plan operating within parameters; include margin for normal operating variations; obtain management review and approval; communicate approved plan to operations | Evidence: Documented production plan showing planned rates and campaigns, DOW parameter reference, management approval signature, communicated plan instructions
keyActivities: Review current Design Operating Window parameters
Identify any current DOW limitations
Develop production plan within DOW limits
Include safety margin in plan
Obtain management approval
Communicate plan to operations
Track actual operations against plan
controls.riskAddressed: Production planned outside DOW; equipment operated beyond design limits; unplanned shutdowns from equipment stress; barrier failure
controls.testingApproach: Verify production plan documented; confirm DOW parameters reviewed; check plan rates are within DOW limits; verify management approval obtained

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Manage Abnormal Situations
controls.subProcess: Recognition and Response to Abnormal Conditions
controls.controlName: Identify Abnormal Operating Conditions and Execute Response Procedure
controls.controlStatement: Who: Console Operator, Control Room Personnel, Operations Supervisor | What: Identify when process parameters exceed limits or normal ranges; execute defined response procedure to bring process back to normal operation or safely shut down | When: Upon recognition of abnormal condition | Where: Control room, operating area, process control systems | Why: To prevent process upsets from escalating into safety incidents; to maintain equipment within safe operating envelope | How: Establish clear abnormal condition definitions in operating procedures; train operators on recognition criteria; define specific response actions for each abnormal condition type; execute response per procedure; monitor effectiveness; escalate if condition does not normalize | Evidence: Alarm logs showing abnormal condition detection, operator response documentation, corrective action records, trend data showing condition resolution
keyActivities: Define abnormal conditions in operating procedures
Establish alarm setpoints for parameter limits
Train operators on recognition and response
Monitor for abnormal conditions continuously
Execute defined response procedure
Document response and effectiveness
Escalate if condition does not normalize
controls.riskAddressed: Abnormal conditions not recognized; delayed response allows escalation; wrong corrective action taken; equipment stress or upset develops
controls.testingApproach: Review operating procedure definitions of abnormal conditions; verify alarm setpoints appropriate; observe operator response to simulated abnormal condition; check incident records for abnormal condition handling

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Manage Abnormal Situations
controls.controlName: Investigate and Manage Design Operating Window Exceedances
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Engineer, Operations Manager, HSSE Lead | What: Investigate any exceedance of Design Operating Window (DOW) parameters; assess impact on equipment integrity; implement interim measures if needed; document findings and actions | When: Immediately upon discovery of DOW exceedance | Where: Investigation documentation, equipment inspection records | Why: To assess whether equipment degradation occurred; to determine if interim safety measures needed before normal operations resume | How: Upon detection, assess magnitude and duration of exceedance; inspect affected equipment for damage; evaluate impact on barriers or integrity; determine if interim measures (reduced rates, enhanced monitoring) needed; plan corrective action; document findings in investigation report; obtain management approval for resuming normal operations | Evidence: Investigation report documenting exceedance details and impact assessment, equipment inspection records, interim measure implementation if needed, Statement of Fitness review if required before restart
keyActivities: Detect and document DOW exceedance
Assess magnitude and duration of exceedance
Inspect equipment for damage or degradation
Evaluate impact on process safety barriers
Determine if interim measures needed
Plan corrective actions
Obtain approval before resuming normal operations
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment damage from exceedance not detected; operations resume with degraded equipment; barrier failure during subsequent operations
controls.testingApproach: Review investigation report for completeness; verify equipment inspection conducted; confirm impact assessment documented; check that interim measures defined if needed

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Manage Limits and Alarms
controls.controlName: Establish and Maintain Critical Alarm Setpoints
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Engineer, Instrumentation Specialist, Operations Manager | What: Establish alarm setpoints for critical process parameters; base setpoints on process safety requirements and equipment design limits; document setpoint rationale; update as design changes | When: During initial system design/startup; reviewed/updated per MOC and technical reviews | Where: Alarm documentation, control system configuration, operating procedures | Why: To ensure timely detection of abnormal conditions before escalation; to provide operators with clear indication of process boundaries | How: Identify critical parameters requiring alarms; calculate appropriate setpoints based on design limits with safety margin; configure alarms in DCS/PLC; document setpoint basis and assumptions; obtain technical review and approval; train operators on alarm meaning and response | Evidence: Documented alarm setpoints with calculation basis, engineering analysis supporting setpoint values, alarm configuration records in control system, operator training records
keyActivities: Identify critical process parameters
Calculate alarm setpoints with safety margin
Document setpoint basis and assumptions
Configure alarms in control system
Obtain technical review and approval
Train operators on alarms and responses
Periodically verify alarm accuracy
controls.riskAddressed: Alarm setpoints incorrect; operators receive no warning of abnormal conditions; process upset escalates; equipment damage or release occurs
controls.testingApproach: Review alarm setpoint documentation for completeness; verify calculation basis provided; confirm alarms configured in control system; check operator training on alarms

controls.controlName: Establish Proactive Technical Monitoring Program
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Engineer, Instrument Technician, HSSE Lead | What: Develop comprehensive proactive monitoring plan to detect equipment degradation before failure; include monitoring methodology, frequency, and acceptance criteria | When: During system design; updated per MOC or condition monitoring findings | Where: Proactive Monitoring Plan document, monitoring procedures | Why: To provide early warning of equipment degradation; to enable preventive action before failure occurs; to maintain integrity of safety barriers | How: Identify critical equipment requiring monitoring; select appropriate monitoring techniques (vibration, temperature, corrosion); establish monitoring frequency based on risk and failure modes; define acceptance criteria and action limits; assign monitoring responsibility; document plan | Evidence: Documented Proactive Monitoring Plan, equipment-specific monitoring procedures, monitoring acceptance criteria, assigned monitoring responsibilities
keyActivities: Identify equipment requiring proactive monitoring
Select appropriate monitoring techniques
Establish monitoring frequency and locations
Define acceptance criteria and action limits
Develop monitoring procedures
Assign monitoring responsibility
Establish trending and analysis process
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment degradation undetected; failure occurs without warning; preventive maintenance opportunity missed; barrier failure during operation
controls.testingApproach: Review Proactive Monitoring Plan for completeness; verify critical equipment identified; check monitoring frequency and methodology appropriate; confirm acceptance criteria defined

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Safe Operation of Storage Tanks
controls.controlName: Establish and Monitor Storage Tank Operating Limits
controls.controlStatement: Who: Tank Operations Supervisor, Control Room Operator, Process Engineer | What: Establish maximum safe fill levels and operating conditions for storage tanks; monitor continuously to prevent overfill or unsafe conditions; implement shutoff systems to prevent overflow | When: Upon tank startup and continuously throughout operations | Where: Tank operating procedures, control system configuration, tank level monitoring systems | Why: To prevent tank overflow which could result in environmental release or personnel hazard; to maintain tank structural integrity | How: Establish maximum safe operating level based on tank capacity and design; install high-level alarms at appropriate level; install low-flow or shutoff systems to automatically terminate inlet flow if high level reached; train operators on level limits; monitor continuously; respond to alarms; document overfill events and corrective actions | Evidence: Documented tank operating procedure with level limits, control system alarm configuration at appropriate levels, operator training on tank level management, incident-free operation or documented response to high-level events
keyActivities: Establish maximum safe operating level for tank
Install high-level alarm at 80-90% capacity
Install low-flow/shutoff valve for overflow prevention
Configure control system to monitor tank level continuously
Train operators on level management and alarm response
Respond to high-level alarms promptly
Document any overfill events and responses
controls.riskAddressed: Tank overfills without detection; environmental release occurs; product loss; operator hazard from overflow; structural damage to tank
controls.testingApproach: Verify maximum level limits documented; confirm high-level alarm installed and set appropriately; test low-flow/shutoff valve function; observe operator knowledge of level limits; check incident records for overfill events

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Safe Operation of Storage Tanks
controls.controlName: Conduct Periodic Tank Inspection and Integrity Assessment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Tank Inspector, Maintenance Engineer, Operations Manager | What: Conduct periodic visual inspection and internal/external assessment of storage tanks to identify corrosion, cracks, foundation issues, or other degradation; assess remaining safe operating life; plan repairs or replacement | When: Annually visual inspection; internal inspection every 3-5 years depending on product corrosivity and tank age | Where: Tank inspection records, tank condition documentation | Why: To detect tank degradation before failure; to ensure tank remains safe for continued operation; to plan maintenance and replacements | How: Conduct visual external inspection for corrosion, sediment, staining, foundation issues; conduct internal inspection using appropriate method (visual if empty, ultrasonic wall thickness if in service); document observations and measurements; assess condition against acceptance criteria; if condition marginal or poor, plan repairs or replacement; document findings | Evidence: Tank inspection reports with observations and measurements, photo documentation if significant conditions noted, condition assessment with accepted/rejected determination, repair/replacement plans if needed
keyActivities: Conduct visual external inspection of tank
Assess foundation and support condition
Conduct internal inspection if tank empty/accessible
Measure wall thickness if in-service inspection
Document observations and measurements
Assess condition against acceptance standards
Plan repairs or replacement if needed
controls.riskAddressed: Tank corrosion or cracking undetected; tank failure occurs; environmental release; product loss; safety hazard
controls.testingApproach: Verify inspection conducted at required frequency; review inspection reports for completeness; confirm measurements documented; check condition assessment against standards; verify repairs/replacement planned if condition poor

controls.controlName: Establish Control of Work Process for All Operational and Maintenance Activities
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work Control Coordinator, Operations Manager, HSSE Lead | What: Establish formal process for planning, approving, and executing all operational and maintenance work activities; ensure work planned reflects design and safety requirements; obtain necessary approvals before work starts | When: Integrated into operations management system from startup and continuously maintained | Where: Control of Work procedure, work planning system, work permits | Why: To ensure work activities do not introduce safety hazards or compromise system integrity; to coordinate work activities to prevent interference or conflicts | How: Develop Control of Work procedure defining work types and planning requirements; require risk assessment for significant work; obtain necessary approvals (operations, HSSE, technical) before work authorization; communicate work requirements to personnel; supervise work execution; verify work completion meets approved plan | Evidence: Documented Control of Work procedure with approval authority structure, completed work permits for significant activities, supervision records, work completion verification
keyActivities: Develop comprehensive Control of Work procedure
Define work types and planning requirements
Require risk assessment for significant work
Establish approval authority structure
Create work permit for significant activities
Communicate work requirements to personnel
Verify work completion per approved plan
controls.riskAddressed: Work proceeds without planning or approval; unplanned system modifications; interference between concurrent work activities; safety incidents during work
controls.testingApproach: Verify Control of Work procedure established and in use; review work permits for completeness of planning; confirm necessary approvals obtained; observe work supervision and completion verification

controls.subProcess: Work Risk Assessment and Hazard Control
controls.controlName: Assess Work-Related Hazards and Implement Controls Before Work Starts
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work Supervisor, HSSE Lead, Authorized Person | What: Identify hazards associated with planned work activity (confined space, hot work, energy sources, chemical hazards); implement required controls; verify controls in place before work authorization | When: During work planning for all significant maintenance and operational tasks | Where: Work risk assessment documentation, work permit, hazard control checklists | Why: To ensure workers are protected from work-related hazards; to prevent incidents during work execution | How: Identify work-specific hazards (confined spaces requiring atmospheric testing, hot work requiring fire watch, energy isolation, chemical hazards, mechanical hazards); specify required controls (permits, equipment, monitoring, personnel); verify controls in place before work starts; maintain controls throughout work duration | Evidence: Work risk assessment documentation, hazard identification and control specification, verified control implementation before work start, supervision documentation maintaining controls
keyActivities: Identify work-specific hazards
Determine required control measures for each hazard
Specify required work permits (hot work, confined space, etc.)
Identify required monitoring and equipment
Specify required personnel qualifications
Verify all controls in place before work authorization
Maintain controls throughout work duration
controls.riskAddressed: Work hazards not identified; inadequate controls implemented; worker injury during work; uncontrolled chemical or energy release during work
controls.testingApproach: Review work risk assessment for completeness of hazard identification; verify appropriate permits obtained for work type; confirm required controls implemented; observe work supervision ensuring controls maintained

controls.controlName: Implement Work Authorization and Permit Systems for Controlled Work
controls.controlStatement: Who: Work Supervisor, Authorized Permit Issuer, Operations Manager | What: For high-risk work activities (confined space, hot work, energy isolation, chemical handling), issue formal work permit documenting all required controls; maintain permit throughout work duration; sign off upon completion | When: For all identified high-risk work activities | Where: Work permit forms, work planning system, archived permits | Why: To document that required controls identified and in place before hazardous work proceeds; to provide record of work authorization | How: Establish clear criteria defining work requiring permits; develop permit forms specific to work type (hot work permit, confined space permit, energy isolation form); require complete hazard identification and control specification on permit; obtain approvals from operations and HSSE before permit issuance; display permit at work site; maintain throughout work duration; sign off upon completion | Evidence: Issued and completed work permits documenting hazards and controls, approval signatures, supervision records, signed permit closure
keyActivities: Define work types requiring formal work permits
Develop permit forms specific to work types
Complete hazard identification and control specification
Obtain required approvals before permit issuance
Display permit at work site during activity
Maintain permit records throughout work
Sign off permit upon work completion
controls.riskAddressed: High-risk work proceeds without documented hazard control; required controls not verified; incidents during high-risk work; accountability unclear
controls.testingApproach: Verify permit system established for high-risk work types; review issued permits for completeness of hazard control specification; confirm approvals obtained before work authorization; check that permits displayed at work sites

controls.controlName: Establish Maritime Safety Requirements Framework and Risk Categories
controls.controlStatement: Who: Maritime Technical Function (MTF) and Content Focal Point | What: Define seven maritime risk categories (cargo vessels, support vessels, ports/berths, personnel vessels, offshore facilities, fleet operations, capital projects) and establish default applicability statements for requirements | When: During SEAM standards adoption and reviewed annually | Where: SEAM Transport Safety Standard, Maritime Safety requirements documentation | Why: To ensure clear understanding of which maritime safety requirements apply to each risk category and facility type | How: Map each of 23 maritime requirements to applicable risk categories using consistent applicability statements (default and bespoke); create matrix showing requirement-to-category relationship; document intent statements explaining regulatory alignment and industry standards adoption; publish requirement guidance through SPF Digital Requirements Library | Evidence: Published SEAM Transport Safety Standard with Figure 1 requirements matrix, default applicability statements for each category, requirement-to-category mapping documentation
keyActivities: Identify all maritime risk categories and scenarios
Define default applicability statements for each category
Map 23 requirements across 7 risk categories
Document intent statements and industry standard alignment
Publish guidance matrix and make accessible through SPF DRL
controls.riskAddressed: Confusion over which requirements apply; inconsistent interpretation across business units; non-compliance with applicable requirements; gaps in coverage of maritime scenarios
controls.testingApproach: Verify SEAM standards published and accessible; confirm all risk categories defined with clear applicability; validate requirement matrix shows correct mapping for applicable risk types; check that SPF DRL navigation reflects maritime categories

controls.controlName: Develop Maritime-Specific Facility Emergency Response Plans
controls.controlStatement: Who: Facility Manager, HSSE Lead, Emergency Response Planning Team | What: Develop comprehensive emergency response plan addressing maritime-specific hazards (vessel collisions, cargo spills, loss of station-keeping, power loss, personnel emergencies) and integrate with facility safety management system | When: Upon initial facility development and updated per major modifications or lessons learned | Where: Facility Emergency Response Plan document, integrated with general emergency procedures | Why: To ensure facility personnel and vessel crews can respond effectively to maritime emergencies with clear roles, communication protocols, and coordinated actions | How: Identify maritime emergency scenarios specific to facility location and operations; define response procedures for each scenario; establish notification protocols to vessel operators; define roles for facility and vessel personnel; integrate with emergency command center; include provisions for external support coordination; train all personnel on plans | Evidence: Documented facility emergency response plan with maritime scenarios, communication protocols, personnel roles and responsibilities, coordination procedures with vessel operators, training records
keyActivities: Identify maritime emergency scenarios (collisions, spills, power loss, station-keeping)
Define response procedures for each scenario
Establish vessel operator notification and coordination protocols
Define facility and vessel personnel roles and responsibilities
Develop training curriculum and conduct initial training
controls.riskAddressed: Maritime emergency occurs without coordinated response; facility and vessel personnel unaware of roles; communication breakdowns; delayed or ineffective response leading to escalation
controls.testingApproach: Review emergency response plan for completeness of maritime scenarios; verify vessel operator coordination procedures documented; confirm personnel training completed; observe emergency drill to validate response execution

controls.controlName: Appoint Maritime Design Expert (MDE) for Capital Projects
controls.controlStatement: Who: Project Manager, Technical Authority, Maritime Design Expert | What: Engage qualified Maritime Design Expert early in capital project definition to evaluate design against SEAM maritime standards and industry practices for vessels, berths, and facilities with maritime interfaces | When: During project initiation for any project with maritime elements | Where: Project documentation, MDE engagement letter, design review records | Why: To ensure maritime-specific design requirements are integrated from conception; to prevent costly design rework and compliance gaps | How: Identify maritime aspects of project scope early; select and appoint qualified MDE with relevant vessel or port facility experience; include MDE in design phases (concept, FEED, detailed design); conduct maritime design reviews; document MDE concurrence on key decisions; obtain technical authority endorsement of maritime design elements | Evidence: MDE engagement documentation, design review meeting minutes with MDE participation, documented MDE concurrence on key design elements, technical authority endorsement record
keyActivities: Identify maritime aspects of project scope in initiation phase
Define MDE qualifications and experience requirements
Engage MDE and establish review schedule
Conduct maritime design reviews at key project phases
Obtain technical authority endorsement of maritime design
controls.riskAddressed: Maritime design requirements not addressed in capital projects; non-compliant design delivered; costly rework required during construction; operational safety compromised
controls.testingApproach: Verify MDE engaged for project with maritime elements; review engagement letter and qualifications; confirm design reviews conducted at required project phases; check technical authority endorsement

controls.controlName: Establish and Monitor Station-Keeping Capability and Environmental Operating Limits
controls.controlStatement: Who: Vessel Master, Dynamic Positioning (DP) Officer, Offshore Facility Manager | What: Establish documented environmental operating limits (weather, sea state, current conditions) for safe station-keeping; verify vessel DP capability meets facility requirements; monitor real-time conditions against limits; implement hold-off procedures when limits are approached | When: Prior to commencing offshore operations and continuously throughout operations | Where: Station-keeping procedure documentation, environmental monitoring system, vessel DP certification records | Why: To prevent loss of station-keeping and consequent vessel collision with facility or equipment | How: Conduct station-keeping analysis assessing vessel DP capability, environmental conditions at facility location, and acceptable risk; define environmental operating limits based on DP analysis and vessel specifications; establish monitoring protocols; implement automated alerts when conditions approach limits; define hold-off or departure procedures; train DP operators on limits and response actions | Evidence: Documented station-keeping analysis with vessel DP capability assessment, environmental operating limits specification, monitoring logs showing continuous observation, alert system records, DP operator training records
keyActivities: Conduct station-keeping analysis assessing DP capability
Define environmental operating limits based on analysis
Establish real-time environmental monitoring system
Implement automated alert system for limit approaches
Train DP operators on limits and hold-off procedures
controls.riskAddressed: Vessel loses station-keeping in adverse conditions; collision occurs with facility or equipment; personnel or asset damage; environmental release
controls.testingApproach: Review station-keeping analysis documentation; verify DP capability certification current; check monitoring logs for continuous environmental tracking; observe alert system function; interview DP operators on environmental limits knowledge

controls.controlName: Collect, Verify, and Maintain Current Hydrographic Information
controls.controlStatement: Who: Port Authority Liaison, Hydrographic Specialist, Navigation Officer | What: Obtain current hydrographic survey data for port approach and berth areas; verify accuracy and currency of water depth information; maintain updates reflecting seasonal or structural changes | When: Prior to vessel approach and regularly updated as changes occur | Where: Hydrographic survey records, nautical charts, pilotage information, vessel navigation systems | Why: To provide accurate water depth data enabling safe navigation planning and to prevent vessel grounding due to outdated depth information | How: Obtain latest official hydrographic surveys from port authorities; cross-reference with recent vessel transit reports; establish data refresh schedule; communicate depth restrictions to pilots and vessel masters; incorporate into vessel passage planning; notify all stakeholders of significant depth changes | Evidence: Current hydrographic survey documentation with date and authority source, vessel navigation system charts updated with latest data, communication records of depth information provided to pilots, records of depth change notifications
keyActivities: Obtain official hydrographic survey from port authority
Verify data currency and compare with recent vessel reports
Establish data refresh schedule and maintenance protocol
Communicate depth information to pilots and vessel masters
Update vessel navigation systems with latest hydrographic data
controls.riskAddressed: Vessel grounds due to reliance on outdated depth information; navigational error from inaccurate water depth data; under-keel clearance violation
controls.testingApproach: Review hydrographic survey documentation for date and authority; verify currency by comparing with recent vessel transit reports; confirm vessel charts updated with current data; check communication records of depth information distribution

controls.subProcess: Mooring System Integrity and Limit Management
controls.controlName: Inspect and Verify Mooring System Capability and Operational Limits
controls.controlStatement: Who: Berth Manager, Mooring Inspection Engineer, Facility Maintenance Team | What: Conduct periodic inspections of all mooring equipment (bollards, fairleads, shackles, lines) to verify integrity and confirm capability for intended vessel loads; establish and communicate operational limits | When: Per scheduled maintenance (typically annually) and before resuming operations after extended downtime | Where: Mooring system inspection records, facility technical documentation, berth operational procedures | Why: To ensure mooring system can safely restrain vessels in service and prevent mooring failure that could lead to vessel collision, release, or personnel injury | How: Conduct visual and measured inspection of all mooring components per engineering standards; test critical components (proof load testing of shackles, wire rope testing); document condition and capability; assess against vessel load requirements for berth; establish and clearly communicate mooring operational limits (maximum vessel size, displacement, loading condition); update facility staff on limits | Evidence: Completed mooring inspection reports with measurement and test results, documented mooring system capability assessment, operational limits documented and communicated to vessel masters and port operators
keyActivities: Conduct visual inspection of all mooring components
Measure critical dimensions and wear indicators
Perform proof load or functional testing of critical items
Assess mooring capability for intended vessel loads
Document operational limits and communicate to stakeholders
controls.riskAddressed: Mooring system components degraded and unable to safely restrain vessel; mooring failure during operation; vessel collision or escape; personnel injury
controls.testingApproach: Review mooring inspection records for completeness; verify measurements documented and compared to acceptance criteria; confirm test results recorded; check operational limits communicated to vessel masters

controls.subProcess: Cargo Transfer Procedure and Quality Assurance
controls.controlName: Establish and Execute Cargo Transfer Operations Procedures with Quality Monitoring
controls.controlStatement: Who: Terminal Operator, Cargo Supervisor, Quality Inspector, Vessel Master | What: Develop detailed cargo transfer procedures addressing product specifications, rate limits, pressure limits, temperature monitoring, and quality verification; execute procedures with continuous monitoring to ensure product quality and safety | When: For each cargo transfer operation | Where: Cargo transfer procedure manual, operation shift procedures, quality monitoring logs, vessel-facility communication records | Why: To prevent cargo contamination, loss of quality specification, pressure/temperature exceedances, or environmental release during transfer | How: Develop comprehensive transfer procedure for each product type and vessel configuration; establish rate limits and pressure/temperature limits based on equipment design and product properties; assign quality inspector to monitor transfer and verify specification compliance; maintain real-time communication between vessel and facility control rooms; record all transfer parameters; stop transfer if parameters exceed limits; conduct final quality verification before closing transfer | Evidence: Documented cargo transfer procedures for each product type, operational rate and limit specifications, real-time transfer monitoring records, quality inspection reports, records of any parameter exceedances and corrective actions
keyActivities: Develop detailed cargo transfer procedures by product type
Establish rate limits and pressure/temperature specifications
Assign quality inspector to monitor transfer continuously
Maintain real-time vessel-facility communication
Conduct final quality verification before transfer closure
controls.riskAddressed: Off-specification product transferred; pressure or temperature limits exceeded; product release or contamination; loss of cargo quality affecting downstream processing
controls.testingApproach: Review transfer procedures for completeness; verify monitoring records show continuous observation; confirm quality inspector present during transfer; check that parameter exceedances documented with corrective actions

controls.controlName: Develop and Maintain Comprehensive Spill Response Plans
controls.controlStatement: Who: HSSE Lead, Emergency Response Coordinator, Spill Response Team Lead | What: Develop facility-specific emergency spill response plan addressing spill scenarios, response procedures, resource allocation, communication protocols, and external authority coordination | When: Upon facility startup and updated biennial or following major incident | Where: Spill Response Plan document integrated in facility HEMP | Why: To enable rapid, coordinated response to minimize environmental impact and demonstrate preparedness to regulatory authorities | How: Conduct spill hazard assessment identifying potential spill scenarios (volume, product type, location); define response procedures for each scenario; allocate equipment and personnel resources; establish communication chain to facility management, corporate, and external authorities; integrate with facility emergency response; obtain approvals from local authorities | Evidence: Current spill response plan with approved scenarios, documented resource allocation, communication protocols, authority approvals, version control and review date
keyActivities: Conduct spill hazard assessment for facility products
Develop response procedures for identified scenarios
Allocate spill response equipment and consumables
Establish communication protocol and authority notifications
Obtain regulatory authority approval of plan
controls.riskAddressed: Spill response capability inadequate; response delayed due to lack of planning; environmental damage from uncontrolled release; regulatory non-compliance
controls.testingApproach: Verify spill response plan current and approved; review scenarios identified reflect facility hazards; confirm equipment and personnel resources allocated; check authority notifications completed

controls.controlName: Maintain Spill Response Equipment and Consumables at Full Readiness
controls.controlStatement: Who: Environmental Coordinator, Equipment Technician, Logistics Manager | What: Maintain inventory and condition of all spill response equipment (booms, skimmers, dispersants, storage containers, communication gear); verify functionality and ensure consumables current; conduct equipment drills quarterly | When: Continuously throughout year; drills quarterly minimum | Where: Equipment storage locations, maintenance logs, readiness inspection records | Why: To ensure equipment available and functional when spill response required; to maintain team proficiency | How: Establish equipment inventory register with location and status; conduct quarterly inventory verification; inspect equipment for damage or degradation; test functional equipment quarterly; rotate consumables per expiration; conduct quarterly deployment drills exercising equipment and team | Evidence: Equipment inventory register with location and status, quarterly inspection and test records, consumable expiration tracking, deployment drill records with participation lists
keyActivities: Maintain equipment inventory register with locations
Conduct quarterly equipment inspection and functionality tests
Track consumable expiration dates and rotate stock
Execute quarterly deployment drills with response team
Document all maintenance and drill activities
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment unavailable or non-functional when needed; team unfamiliar with equipment; response capability degraded; consumables expired and ineffective
controls.testingApproach: Verify inventory register current and accurate; inspect equipment at registered locations; check quarterly inspection/test records; review consumable rotation logs; observe deployment drill execution

controls.controlName: Establish Notification Protocol and Escalation Procedures
controls.controlStatement: Who: Discovery Personnel, Shift Supervisor, HSSE Lead, Management Chain | What: Upon discovery of spill or uncontrolled release, immediately notify supervisor and HSSE lead; escalate through defined chain based on spill magnitude and activate response procedures | When: Immediately upon discovery of spill | Where: Communication system (radio, phone, alarm system), facility emergency procedures | Why: To ensure rapid response initiation and appropriate resource mobilization; to meet regulatory notification timelines | How: Establish notification protocol defining discovery notification triggers (spill detected, confirmed release); define escalation chain and decision criteria for response activation; train all personnel on notification process; establish communication redundancy; document all notifications with timestamps; maintain regulatory notification logs | Evidence: Documented notification protocol in facility procedures, training records for notification procedure, communication system verification (redundancy testing), notification logs showing timely escalation
keyActivities: Define spill discovery notification triggers and process
Establish escalation chain with decision criteria
Train all facility personnel on notification procedure
Test communication system redundancy quarterly
Maintain notification logs with timestamps for regulatory compliance
controls.riskAddressed: Spill response delayed due to communication breakdown; notification not escalated appropriately; regulatory reporting timelines missed; uncontrolled release duration extends
controls.testingApproach: Review notification protocol in facility procedures; verify training completed for all personnel; observe communication system functionality; check notification logs for proper escalation and timing

controls.controlName: Conduct Periodic Spill Response Exercises and Drills
controls.controlStatement: Who: Emergency Response Coordinator, Facility Manager, Response Team Members | What: Execute spill response exercises testing activation procedures, personnel response, equipment deployment, and coordination with external agencies; document performance and improvement areas | When: Minimum annually; tabletop annually, full-scale drill biennial | Where: Facility grounds (full-scale) or meeting room (tabletop), exercise documentation | Why: To verify response capability, identify gaps, and maintain team proficiency | How: Develop exercise scenario from facility spill hazards; conduct tabletop exercise annually involving management and key response personnel; execute biennial full-scale deployment exercise with actual equipment deployment; observe exercise noting response timing, issues, and effectiveness; conduct after-action review documenting findings and corrective actions | Evidence: Documented exercise scenarios, exercise execution records (photos, observer notes), after-action review documentation with improvement actions, corrective action completion tracking
keyActivities: Develop exercise scenario based on facility hazards
Conduct annual tabletop exercise with management and response leads
Execute biennial full-scale deployment exercise
Document exercise observations and response performance
Conduct after-action review and identify improvements
controls.riskAddressed: Response capability not validated; team unfamiliar with procedures under pressure; equipment deployment process not tested; response performance issues unknown until actual incident
controls.testingApproach: Verify annual tabletop exercise documentation; confirm biennial full-scale exercise executed; review after-action reports and improvement actions; check that prior year recommendations implemented

controls.controlName: Provide Spill Response Training and Maintain Response Team Competency
controls.controlStatement: Who: Training Coordinator, Response Team Supervisor, Response Team Members | What: Provide initial and refresher training on spill response procedures, equipment operation, and safety protocols; maintain competency validation for response team personnel | When: Initial training for new team members; refresher training annually minimum | Where: Training venue, response training records, competency assessment records | Why: To ensure response team members can execute procedures safely and effectively when called upon | How: Develop response training curriculum covering facility scenarios, procedure execution, equipment operation, personal protective equipment, and communication protocols; conduct initial training for all response team members; maintain current competency through annual refresher training; assess competency through observation and practical exercises; address performance gaps with additional training | Evidence: Training curriculum documentation, training attendance records with dates and names, competency assessment records, practical exercise performance documentation
keyActivities: Develop spill response training curriculum
Conduct initial training for response team members
Provide annual refresher training on procedures and equipment
Assess competency through practical exercises
Address performance gaps with targeted training
controls.riskAddressed: Response team members untrained or incompetent; response actions executed incorrectly; unnecessary delays or ineffective response; personnel safety during response compromised
controls.testingApproach: Review training curriculum and materials; verify training completion records; assess team member knowledge during interview; observe team member performance during exercise

controls.controlName: Conduct After-Action Reviews of Spill Response Events
controls.controlStatement: Who: Incident Investigation Team, HSSE Lead, Response Team Members | What: Following any actual spill response or failed control leading to uncontrolled release, conduct structured after-action review examining response execution, effectiveness, and opportunities for improvement | When: Within 7 days of incident completion | Where: Investigation documentation, meeting records, improvement tracking system | Why: To capture learnings and implement improvements preventing recurrence or improving response effectiveness | How: Assemble investigation team including response participants; document incident timeline and response actions; assess response against plan; identify what went well and what could improve; develop corrective actions addressing root causes; communicate learnings to response team and facility personnel; track improvement actions to closure | Evidence: Incident investigation report documenting event timeline and response, assessment of response effectiveness, identified improvement opportunities, documented corrective actions with ownership and completion tracking
keyActivities: Document incident timeline and response actions taken
Assess response effectiveness against plan
Identify improvement opportunities and root causes
Develop corrective actions addressing findings
Track improvements to closure and communicate learnings
controls.riskAddressed: Incident response weaknesses not identified; similar incidents recur; organizational capability not improved; response team morale affected by lack of follow-through
controls.testingApproach: Verify after-action review completed within required timeframe; review investigation report for completeness; confirm corrective actions assigned and tracked; check that learnings communicated to response team

controls.controlName: Conduct Independent Assurance of Spill Response Preparedness
controls.controlStatement: Who: Independent HSSE Auditor, Technical Authority, Assurance Coordinator | What: Conduct independent assessment of spill response capability including plan adequacy, equipment readiness, personnel training, and exercise performance | When: Biennial minimum as part of HSSE assurance program | Where: Assurance audit documentation, facility records, equipment locations | Why: To provide independent verification that spill response capability meets regulatory and company standards | How: Develop risk-based assurance plan; conduct document review of response plan, training records, exercise documentation; perform equipment inspection and functionality verification; interview response team members on capabilities; assess plan currency and completeness; document findings and recommendations; track corrective actions to closure | Evidence: Assurance audit plan, document review findings, equipment inspection records, personnel interview notes, audit report with findings and recommendations, corrective action tracking
keyActivities: Review spill response plan for completeness and currency
Verify response equipment inventory and functionality
Assess training completion and competency validation
Evaluate exercise program scope and effectiveness
Interview response team on knowledge and readiness
controls.riskAddressed: Response capability degradation not detected; non-compliance with standards not identified; inadequate response capability remains unaddressed; assurance gap exists
controls.testingApproach: Verify assurance conducted at required frequency; review audit findings for completeness; confirm corrective actions identified and tracked; check equipment and training verify with audit findings

controls.controlName: Establish OT Security Governance Structure and Define Roles
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Leadership, HSSE Lead, OT Security Manager, Technical Authority | What: Establish facility-level OT security governance including executive responsibility for OT security, definition of OT security roles and responsibilities, and integration of OT security into asset management system | When: During asset startup and updated when organizational structure changes | Where: Governance documentation, organizational charts, role definitions | Why: To ensure clear accountability for OT security; to establish management oversight and continuous improvement | How: Define executive sponsor responsible for OT security governance; appoint OT Security Manager with authority and resources; establish OT security team including engineering, operations, and IT representation; define roles and responsibilities for risk assessment, control implementation, monitoring, and incident response; integrate OT security into asset management governance; establish regular governance meetings to review performance and risks | Evidence: Documented governance structure with executive responsibility, OT Security Manager appointment, team composition, documented role definitions, governance meeting minutes, performance monitoring reports
keyActivities: Define executive responsibility for OT security governance
Appoint OT Security Manager with appropriate authority
Establish OT security team with cross-functional representation
Document role definitions and responsibilities
Establish governance meeting schedule and performance review
controls.riskAddressed: OT security accountability unclear; no single owner responsible; poor coordination across functions; OT security risk not elevated to leadership attention
controls.testingApproach: Verify governance structure documented and communicated; confirm OT Security Manager appointed and present; review governance meeting minutes for regular occurrence; check that OT security integrated into asset management reviews

controls.subProcess: OT Asset Inventory and Network Architecture
controls.controlName: Maintain Current OT Asset Inventory and Network Architecture Documentation
controls.controlStatement: Who: IT Systems Administrator, OT Security Manager, OT Engineer | What: Maintain comprehensive inventory of all OT assets (controllers, PLCs, sensors, actuators, networks) and document complete network architecture showing data flow and system interconnections | When: Upon asset startup and updated whenever OT systems change | Where: Asset management system, network documentation, configuration management database | Why: To provide foundation for security risk assessment and to enable detection of unauthorized devices or configuration changes | How: Conduct comprehensive OT asset discovery and document all devices, their location, function, network connections, and firmware versions; create network architecture diagrams showing all OT systems and interconnections; establish change control process requiring inventory and documentation updates for any OT system additions or modifications; maintain version control; periodically validate inventory against physical systems | Evidence: Current OT asset inventory with device details and locations, network architecture diagrams showing system interconnections, change control records documenting inventory updates, periodic validation records
keyActivities: Conduct comprehensive OT asset discovery and identification
Document device specifications, locations, and firmware versions
Create network architecture diagrams
Establish change control process for OT systems
Periodically validate inventory against physical systems
controls.riskAddressed: Unknown OT assets present; rogue devices undetected; network architecture unclear; vulnerability assessment incomplete; asset criticality unknown
controls.testingApproach: Review inventory documentation; verify against physical systems through spot-check; review network diagrams for completeness; confirm change control records for OT modifications

controls.subProcess: Management of Change for OT Security
controls.controlName: Implement MOC Process for OT Systems to Assess Security Impacts
controls.controlStatement: Who: MOC Coordinator, OT Security Manager, Engineering Lead | What: Ensure all changes to OT systems (firmware updates, network changes, new equipment, configuration changes) are evaluated for security impact before implementation | When: For all OT system changes | Where: MOC documentation, security impact assessment records | Why: To prevent introduction of security vulnerabilities through changes; to maintain network segmentation and access controls | How: Establish MOC process requiring security impact assessment for all OT changes; define security review criteria (network isolation impacts, firmware source validation, testing requirements); require security team approval before change implementation; document change details and security assessment; verify change execution matches approved plan; track and verify restoration of security controls post-change | Evidence: MOC procedure document with OT security requirements, security impact assessment templates, approval records showing security review completion, change verification documentation
keyActivities: Develop MOC process including security impact assessment
Define security review criteria for OT changes
Require security team approval before implementation
Document and verify change execution
Verify security controls restored after change completion
controls.riskAddressed: OT security controls bypassed through changes; network segmentation compromised; unauthorized firmware installed; security vulnerabilities introduced
controls.testingApproach: Review MOC procedure for security assessment requirements; verify security team approval on recent OT changes; confirm security impact documented; check that controls verified post-implementation

controls.controlName: Implement Network Segmentation Separating OT Systems from IT Networks
controls.controlStatement: Who: Network Engineer, IT Security Manager, OT Security Manager | What: Implement and maintain network segmentation isolating OT systems from general IT networks and the internet; implement firewall rules allowing only required communication flows | When: Upon asset startup and continuously maintained | Where: Network infrastructure, firewall configuration, network diagrams | Why: To limit impact of IT security incidents on OT operations; to prevent unauthorized remote access to OT systems; to control data flows between OT and IT networks | How: Implement air-gapped network architecture separating OT systems from IT; deploy firewalls at OT-IT boundary with default deny-all firewall rules; define and document required communication flows (industrial data, remote monitoring); configure firewall rules to allow only documented flows; implement intrusion detection for attempted OT-IT unauthorized access; monitor firewall logs for policy violations | Evidence: Network architecture diagram showing segmentation, firewall configuration documentation with rules, communication matrix defining allowed flows, firewall log monitoring records, intrusion detection alerts
keyActivities: Design network segmentation architecture for OT isolation
Implement firewalls at OT-IT boundary
Define required OT-IT communication flows
Configure firewall rules for allowed communications only
Monitor firewall logs and intrusion detection for violations
controls.riskAddressed: OT systems accessible from IT network; malware propagation from IT to OT; unauthorized remote access to OT; uncontrolled data flows between networks
controls.testingApproach: Review network architecture and segmentation design; verify firewall rules configured correctly; test firewall filtering with authorized and unauthorized traffic; review firewall logs for policy violations

controls.controlName: Implement Access Controls and Limit User Privileges to Necessary Functions
controls.controlStatement: Who: IT Security Manager, OT Security Manager, System Administrator | What: Implement identity and access management controlling who can access OT systems; enforce principle of least privilege limiting user capabilities to minimum required for role | When: Upon user onboarding and whenever role changes | Where: Access control system, user account records, privilege assignment documentation | Why: To prevent unauthorized access to OT systems; to limit damage from compromised credentials; to maintain accountability for system changes | How: Implement user authentication requiring unique credentials and multi-factor authentication for remote access; establish role-based access control limiting capabilities by user role; enforce least privilege principle; eliminate shared or default accounts; implement privileged access management for administrative functions; log and monitor all access; conduct quarterly access reviews removing access no longer needed | Evidence: Access control system configuration, user account records with role and privilege assignments, authentication logs, access review documentation, privileged access management logs
keyActivities: Establish user authentication requirements including MFA
Define role-based access control profiles
Apply least privilege principle to all roles
Eliminate shared and default credentials
Implement privileged access management for admin functions
controls.riskAddressed: Unauthorized user access to OT systems; shared credentials compromise accountability; excessive privileges enable damage from compromised account; former employees retain access
controls.testingApproach: Review access control configuration; verify MFA enabled for remote access; sample user accounts and confirm privilege assignment matches role; confirm shared accounts eliminated; review access logs for unexpected activity

controls.controlName: Implement Vulnerability Assessment and Patch Management Program
controls.controlStatement: Who: OT Security Manager, IT Security Manager, System Administrator | What: Conduct regular vulnerability assessments of OT systems; develop patching strategy balancing security updates with operational stability; implement patch management with rigorous testing before production deployment | When: Vulnerability assessments quarterly; patches applied per criticality (critical within 30 days, high within 60 days) | Where: Vulnerability assessment reports, patch management records, test environment documentation | Why: To identify security vulnerabilities before exploitation; to maintain system security through timely patching; to balance security updates with operational stability | How: Conduct quarterly vulnerability assessments of OT systems (network scanning, application assessment); evaluate patches for applicability and security impact; test patches in isolated test environment matching production configuration; establish patch rollout plan with minimal operational impact; apply patches per schedule; verify patch successful and system functionality maintained; track all patching activities | Evidence: Quarterly vulnerability assessment reports, patch evaluation documentation, test environment validation records, patch deployment logs, system functionality verification post-patching
keyActivities: Conduct quarterly vulnerability assessments of OT systems
Evaluate security impact and applicability of patches
Test patches in isolated test environment first
Develop minimal-impact deployment plan
Verify patch effectiveness and system functionality post-deployment
controls.riskAddressed: Known vulnerabilities remain unpatched; security exploits compromise OT systems; patch deployment causes operational disruption; patch testing inadequate
controls.testingApproach: Review vulnerability assessment reports for completeness; verify patches tested before production; check patch deployment logs against schedule; confirm system functionality validated after patching

controls.controlName: Establish Event Logging and Monitoring for OT Security Incidents
controls.controlStatement: Who: IT Security Manager, OT Security Manager, SOC Analyst | What: Implement comprehensive event logging for OT systems capturing security-relevant events; establish 24/7 security monitoring detecting suspicious activity and potential incidents | When: Continuously throughout operations | Where: Security information and event management (SIEM) system, event logs, monitoring dashboards | Why: To detect and respond to security incidents in near real-time; to maintain forensic capability for incident investigations | How: Configure logging on all OT systems capturing authentication events, configuration changes, system errors, and security-related events; forward logs to central SIEM system; establish monitoring rules detecting suspicious patterns (multiple failed logins, unauthorized access attempts, unusual network traffic); implement alerting system notifying security team of suspected incidents; maintain logs for minimum 1 year; establish incident response team on-call for escalation | Evidence: SIEM system configuration with OT system logging, monitoring rule documentation, alert log showing incidents detected, incident response records, log retention verification
keyActivities: Configure security event logging on all OT systems
Forward logs to centralized SIEM for analysis
Establish monitoring rules detecting security incidents
Implement real-time alerting for suspicious activity
Maintain incident response team for escalation
controls.riskAddressed: Security incidents not detected; breach dwell time extended; forensic capability lost; response delayed allowing incident escalation
controls.testingApproach: Verify logging configured on OT systems; confirm logs forwarding to SIEM; test alert rules with simulated incidents; check incident response team established and on-call

controls.controlName: Control Use of Portable Media and External Devices on OT Networks
controls.controlStatement: Who: OT Security Manager, IT Security Manager, Operations Supervisor | What: Implement policy restricting use of USB drives, external hard drives, and other portable media on OT systems; implement technical controls preventing unauthorized device connections; allow only approved devices with encryption and access controls | When: Continuously throughout operations | Where: Device control policy, technical controls (USB blocking, device scanning), approved device registry | Why: To prevent introduction of malware through portable media; to prevent unauthorized data exfiltration from OT systems; to maintain network security | How: Establish policy prohibiting personal portable media on OT systems; implement approved device program allowing only business-required portable media with encryption; configure OT systems to block unauthorized USB device connections; scan approved media before use; maintain inventory of approved devices; periodically audit device usage; enforce policy through discipline and technical controls | Evidence: Device control policy, approved device registry with encryption verification, USB port blocking configuration, device scanning logs, policy violation records
keyActivities: Establish policy restricting portable media on OT systems
Develop approved device program with encryption requirements
Implement technical controls blocking unauthorized devices
Scan all approved media before OT system use
Conduct periodic audits of device usage
controls.riskAddressed: Malware introduced through infected portable media; unauthorized data exfiltration via USB devices; policy violations undetected; infection propagates across OT network
controls.testingApproach: Review device control policy; verify USB ports blocked on OT systems; test blocked port enforcement; scan approved device inventory; review scanning logs

controls.controlName: Conduct OT Security Risk Assessment Identifying Threats and Vulnerabilities
controls.controlStatement: Who: OT Security Manager, Risk Assessment Team, Technical Authority | What: Conduct comprehensive risk assessment of OT environment identifying threat actors, potential attack vectors, vulnerabilities in current controls, and consequences of successful attacks | When: Upon asset startup and reviewed biennially or when major OT changes occur | Where: Risk assessment report, risk register, vulnerability assessments | Why: To understand OT security risk landscape; to prioritize control implementation and improvements; to inform risk mitigation strategy | How: Assemble risk assessment team with OT engineering and IT security expertise; identify threat actors and motivations relevant to facility; assess current attack vectors and vulnerabilities; evaluate effectiveness of existing controls; assess consequences of successful attacks; rate risks using risk matrix; prioritize risks by magnitude; develop risk mitigation plans for high risks | Evidence: Documented risk assessment report with threat and vulnerability analysis, risk ratings and prioritization, risk mitigation plans, technical authority review and approval
keyActivities: Identify potential threat actors and motivations
Assess attack vectors and technical vulnerabilities
Evaluate effectiveness of current security controls
Assess consequences of successful attacks
Rate and prioritize risks for mitigation
